brown_pure.title: Brown noise (pure)
brown_pure.description: Soft, deep noise emphasizing low frequencies (1/f²). Good for relaxation and falling asleep. Often used by people with high-pitched tinnitus.

brown_lp1000.title: Brown lowpass 1000 Hz
brown_lp1000.description: Filtered brown noise — frequencies above 1000 Hz removed. Softer, more ambient sound. Good for long concentration.

brown_lp500.title: Brown lowpass 500 Hz
brown_lp500.description: Brown noise filtered to very low frequencies (below 500 Hz). Feels more like a deep rumble than noise.

pink_pure.title: Pink noise (pure)
pink_pure.description: Natural ambient noise (1/f). Similar to light rain or wind. One of the most studied noise types for sleep.

pink_lp2000.title: Pink lowpass 2000 Hz
pink_lp2000.description: Filtered pink noise — without the hiss of high frequencies. Softer, more pleasant for long listening.

pink_lp4000.title: Pink lowpass 4000 Hz

privacy.title: Privacy policy
privacy.intro: AURALIS respects your privacy. This document explains simply what we (do not) collect and what happens to your data.

collection.title: What we collect
collection.text: AURALIS does NOT collect personal data. Everything runs on your device. No account registration, no email, no phone.

storage.title: Where data is stored
storage.text: All data (quiz answers, journal, favorites, listening history) is stored only in the browser or app on your device (localStorage / app sandbox). When you delete the app, the data disappears with it.

sharing.title: Sharing
sharing.text: We do NOT share data with third parties. No analytics, no advertising trackers, no third-party cookies. No cloud server to send anything to.

gdpr.title: Your rights (GDPR)
gdpr.text: You can download all your data as JSON at any time (Settings → My data → Download everything). You can delete all data with one button. Since we keep nothing on a server, there is nothing to request separately.

platform.title: Google Play / App Store
platform.text: When installed from Google Play or the App Store, the platform may process your email and payment information — that is their responsibility, not ours. Read their policies separately.

privacy.body:
AURALIS is a hearing relaxation tool. It does not cure tinnitus and does not replace medical advice.

All your data (quiz answers, journal, favorites, listening history) is stored locally on your device.

We collect nothing. We do not send any data to a server. No tracking, no analytics, no third-party cookies.

You can delete all your data at any time from Settings → My data → Delete all data.

When installed from Google Play / App Store, the platform may process your email and payment information — that is their responsibility, not ours.

For questions: tiholenev@gmail.com

terms.title: Terms of use
terms.intro: By opening AURALIS you accept these terms. They are short and simple.

wellness.title: Wellness, not a medical app
wellness.text: AURALIS is a hearing relaxation tool. It helps with habituation (getting used) to tinnitus through sound enrichment, breathing and guided meditations. It is NOT a medical device. It does NOT replace a doctor, diagnosis or prescribed therapy.

disclaimer.title: Health disclaimer
disclaimer.text: For intense or sudden tinnitus (especially in one ear, with dizziness or hearing loss), consult an ear-nose-throat (ENT) specialist. If you have thoughts of self-harm or severe depression, seek professional help immediately.

volume.title: Volume
volume.text: Listen at a moderate volume. Do not let the sound completely mask the tinnitus (this would be harmful with long-term use). Recommended level: with headphones, around 30–40% of max.

noWarranty.title: No guarantee of results
noWarranty.text: AURALIS does NOT guarantee a reduction in tinnitus. Results vary by individual. The cited studies are a reference, not a promise.

license.title: Usage license
license.text: With payment you receive a personal right to use AURALIS on your devices. You may not resell, decompile or redistribute the app.

changes.title: Changes
changes.text: We may update the app with new sounds, fixes and features. These Terms may change — you will see the date above with each new version.

volumeCalib.title: Volume setup
volumeCalib.subtitle: Find the level that works for you.
volumeCalib.explain: Sound therapy works best when the background sound is slightly quieter than your tinnitus — the two sounds blend partially rather than masking. This is called the "mixing point" and it's the principle that lets the brain gradually get used to it.
volumeCalib.step1: Sit in a quiet room. Make sure there's no TV, conversation or street noise.
volumeCalib.step2: Play the test sound. Listen at a low level for 20 seconds.
volumeCalib.step3: Lower the volume gradually. Stop the moment you hear both the tinnitus and the background sound at once. They should coexist.
volumeCalib.sliderHint: Adjust to a level you hear comfortably.
volumeCalib.warnBody: Don't turn it up until the tinnitus is fully masked. This strains your hearing and trains the brain to expect complete silence — the opposite of our goal.
volumeCalib.disclaimer: AURALIS is a general wellness tool, not a medical product.

s1.title: Why AURALIS works
s1.subtitle: Scientific basis of the therapy
s1.intro: AURALIS is a digital app for managing chronic subjective tinnitus. It is not a medicine. It does not replace a medical examination. But the methods inside are based on 14 randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and FDA-approved devices.
s1.do1: We measure the severity of your tinnitus with the THI — a clinically validated scale from 1996.
s1.do2: We find your exact tinnitus frequency with a pitch-matching test (binary search).
s1.do3: We remove exactly that frequency from all the therapeutic sounds — this is called "Notched Sound Therapy".
s1.do4: You listen to these sounds for 30–60 minutes a day.

s2.title: THI: what we measure
s2.subtitle: Tinnitus Handicap Inventory (Newman et al., 1996)
paragraphs: The Tinnitus Handicap Inventory (THI) was created in 1996 by Newman, Jacobson and Spitzer — a psychometrically validated questionnaire for assessing tinnitus severity.

items: Functional (11 q.): concentration, sleep, work
Emotional (9 q.): anxiety, anger, frustration
Catastrophic (5 q.): a sense of hopelessness

paragraphs: Points: Yes = 4, Sometimes = 2, No = 0. Total score: 0–100.

items: 0–16: Slight
18–36: Mild
38–56: Moderate
58–76: Severe
78–100: Catastrophic

paragraphs.text: Clinically significant improvement (MCID): a drop of at least 7 points. This is the established threshold for real change.

paragraphs: Why we do it: a measurement BEFORE therapy + a REPEAT after 14 days — so you see objective progress, not just a subjective feeling.

s3.title: Pitch Matching
s3.subtitle: Finding your tinnitus frequency
paragraphs: Tinnitus has a "pitch" — one or several main frequencies. For most people with tonal tinnitus this is between 1000 and 12000 Hz (usually in the mid-high frequencies).
Two-Alternative Forced Choice — a standard psychophysical method for determining threshold frequencies.

items: You hear Tone A and Tone B (two different frequencies).
You choose which is closer to your tinnitus.
The system narrows the range using binary logic.
After 3–4 iterations we find the frequency with an accuracy of ±1/4 octave.

paragraphs.text: Why binary search: log₂(12 possible frequencies) ≈ 3–4 iterations. Minimum taps, maximum accuracy.

s4.title: Notched Therapy
s4.subtitle: The therapeutic heart of AURALIS
paragraphs: With prolonged listening to a sound that has your tinnitus frequency removed, the brain "re-maps" the auditory cortex. The neurons responsible for the phantom tone gradually reduce their activity.

items: Notch filter (Web Audio BiquadFilterNode of type "notch")
Q factor = 2.871 (standard for 1/2 octave bandwidth)
Centered exactly on your pitch frequency

paragraphs: Pantev et al. (2012) — "Tailor-made notched music training for tinnitus", Frontiers in Systems Neuroscience. The first RCT to show a significant reduction after 6 months of daily listening.
Stein et al. (2015) — Repeated follow-up. Confirms long-term effects.

items: 14 RCTs, 793 patients in total
Month 3: −8.6 THI points (95% CI)
Month 6: −24.6 THI points
The therapeutic effect GROWS with long-term use
Better tolerated than TRT (Tinnitus Retraining Therapy)

s5.title: Sound therapy
s5.subtitle: 256 samples, 10 categories, 2 layers
paragraphs: AURALIS uses 256 therapeutic sound samples, organized into 10 categories: Ocean, Rain, River, Underwater, Wind, Meditation, Forest, Fire, Ambient, Noise.

items: Passes through a notch filter (removes your frequency)
Loop-friendly (no crossfade artifacts)
Normalized by LUFS (constant loudness)
Layer 1 (main sound): nature, music, meditation
Layer 2 (background noise): pink or brown (generated in real time)

paragraphs: Why two layers: pink/brown noise provides a constant "masking" base. Natural sounds give an emotionally pleasant experience. The combination increases compliance (continued listening).

items: At least 30 minutes a day
Ideally 60–120 minutes
14-day introductory program
12-week full course for a measurable result
All sounds are normalized below 70 dB SPL (a safe level)
We recommend closed headphones for clarity
Stop listening if you feel discomfort
All your data stays on your device (localStorage)
AURALIS sends nothing to servers
THI answers, pitch results, journal — local only
AURALIS is a digital wellness platform, not a medical device
Does not replace an examination by an ENT doctor
For severe cases (THI > 78) we recommend a consultation

profile_results.title: Your assessment

welcomes.TH_C: You hear a high-pitched, constant whistling or ringing tone that intrudes especially in quiet environments and during mental work. This is a common profile in people who have been exposed to loud sounds or notice age-related changes in hearing — the brain compensates for the missing signals with its own activity. AURALIS will offer you filtered nature sounds and pink noise for masking, along with short CBT practices that steer attention away from the ringing.
welcomes.DN_S: You hear a dense, low-frequency, sometimes buzzing or rumbling noise that is especially intrusive in the evening and disrupts the quality of your sleep. This is a common profile in people who spend time in quiet bedrooms — without external sound the brain amplifies the internal noise. AURALIS will offer you deep brown sounds that blend the tinnitus with a background without masking it completely — this strategy is called the "mixing point" and helps you sleep better without sudden silence.
welcomes.SS_R: Your tinnitus is strongly linked to the state of your nervous system — it intensifies with stress, anxiety and emotional load, and eases when you're calm. This reflects a high sensitivity of the body to signals — your limbic system reacts quickly to changes. AURALIS will give you gentle nature sounds, a 4-7-8 breathing exercise for acute moments, and short acceptance practices that break the vicious cycle between noise and anxiety.
welcomes.SM_F: Your tinnitus has a variable character — its loudness changes throughout the day, sometimes significantly, and it's often linked to physical tension in the neck, jaw or shoulders. This is a somatic type of tinnitus: the muscles around the ear and the trigeminal nerve can modulate the noise. AURALIS will offer you dynamic nature sounds such as ocean waves (which engage the brain more strongly), a jaw-relaxation exercise and gentle massage — body practices have a measurable effect in this profile.
welcomes.HB_M: You have tinnitus, but it bothers you mainly in conditions of complete silence and has no significant impact on your daily tasks, concentration, sleep or mood. Your nervous system has already built a high degree of natural adaptation — your brain largely filters the phantom signal as an unimportant background. AURALIS will offer you ultra-soft nature sounds for moments of total silence and guidance on how to maintain this resilience even during stressful periods.

disclaimer.title: Important — scientific limitations
disclaimer.text: AURALIS is a wellness support tool; it does not replace medical diagnosis or treatment. The recommendations are based on aggregated research and general models, not on a personal medical examination. For severe symptoms or progressive worsening — consult a specialist (ENT doctor or audiologist).

TH_C.description: Profile "Tonal high". A detailed description will be added to this app soon.
TH_C.expectations: Detailed expectations for this profile will be added soon.
TH_C.meaning:
You hear a high-pitched, constant whistling or ringing tone that intrudes especially in quiet environments and during mental work. This type of tinnitus is perceived as a single-tone sound concentrated at the high end of the audible spectrum.

In the clinical model this state is explained through the "contrast enhancement effect": in absolute silence the brain directs maximum cognitive attention to the internal signal, which becomes dominant — like a lit candle in a dark room.

At the cellular level there is a weakening of inhibitory tone in the central auditory system — reduced GABA activity and increased glutamatergic excitation, which creates abnormal synchronization in the auditory cortex. The higher cognitive centers interpret this synchronization as a persistent phantom sound.
TH_C.why: The most common factors are prolonged past exposure to high sound levels (concerts, industrial work, firearms) and age-related damage to the high-frequency hair cells in the cochlea (presbycusis). Contributing factors that increase perception: a quiet environment in the evening, insufficient sleep, high stress levels, dehydration and caffeine.
TH_C.strategyReasoning:
- Recommended carrier sound: filtered rain or balanced pink noise.

- Recommended `recommended_noise` in the library: `pink_lowpass_4000` or `brown_lowpass_1000`.

- Recommended sound/noise ratio: 0.70/0.30 (slightly sound-dominant). The "mixing point" principle — the sound should be slightly below the loudness of the tinnitus, not mask it completely.

- Recommended duration: at least 2 hours a day for stable habituation; ideally 4–6 hours spread throughout the day.

- Top use cases by priority: `daily` (mental work) → `relaxation` → `falling_asleep`.

timeline.expectation: usually a sense of a "less intrusive" sound appears first while listening. Right after the sound stops, the tinnitus is perceived at its original loudness. This is completely normal and does not mean the process isn't working.
studies record an average reduction of about 17.6% on the tinnitus health questionnaire at the fourth week.

a meta-analysis of 14 RCTs shows an average improvement of −8.6 points on the THI scale at month three. The MCID (minimal clinically important difference) for THI is 7 points — so at month 3 many people cross the threshold of a "noticeable" change.
the same meta-analysis records an average improvement of −24.6 points on the THI at month 6, almost 3 times above the MCID. The minimum therapeutic window for stable re-tuning is 9–18 months.

TH_C.additional:
- Listening environment: prefer open speakers or bone conduction over closed headphones — the occlusion effect can amplify the internal sound.

- Sleep hygiene: avoid absolute silence in the bedroom; the contrast between tinnitus and silence is strongest in the evening.

- Caffeine and alcohol: limit caffeine after 2 PM and alcohol in the evening. Caffeine is a central stimulant that increases cortical sensitivity; alcohol changes sleep architecture.

- Cognitive work: for concentration use a `focus_brown_noise`-style mix — brown noise blocks the high-frequency ringing without overloading the mid frequencies important for speech.

- Additional techniques: Cognitive behavioural counselling (CBT) is recommended as a complement to sound therapy — it breaks the "cognitive cycle of distress".
TH_C.medicalFlags:
- Immediately to an ENT: if the tinnitus is one-sided (only in one ear), pulsatile (synchronized with your heartbeat), newly appeared in the last few days, or accompanied by a sudden loss of hearing.

- To an audiologist: if you notice worsening speech intelligibility in noisy environments (restaurant, party) — there may be hidden hearing loss that requires an objective hearing test.

- To a psychologist / psychotherapist: if the tinnitus leads you to persistent thoughts like "I can't take it" or "it will always be like this" — a structured CBT course is proven effective.

reasons.sleep_deep: Deep frequencies against the high tone before sleep
reasons.relaxation: Quiet nature sounds for evening calm

DN_S.description: Profile "Low-frequency noise". A detailed description will be added to this app soon.
DN_S.expectations: Detailed expectations for this profile will be added soon.
DN_S.meaning:
You hear a dense, low-frequency, sometimes buzzing or rumbling noise that is especially intrusive in the evening and disrupts the quality of your sleep. Your complaints most often center on falling asleep and maintaining sleep — waking during the night, a feeling of "not enough rest" in the morning.

In the clinical model, tinnitus and insomnia form a bidirectional closed cycle — each state feeds the other. The limbic system interprets the tinnitus as a threat and activates the HPA axis (hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal), leading to elevated cortisol in the late afternoon and pre-sleep period, keeping the nervous system in hyperarousal.

Polysomnography studies show that in this profile sleep architecture is severely disrupted — on average 85% of the night is spent in light stages 1–2, while deep slow-wave sleep and REM drop to ~6.4% (in healthy controls — 21.5%).
DN_S.why:
This profile is often associated with a low-frequency hydropic or vascular component (for example a one-sided throbbing noise with blood-pressure fluctuations), with prolonged household or work exposure to low noises (ventilation, machinery), or with age-related deafferentation of the low frequencies.

Aggravating factors: evening silence in the bedroom (amplifies the internal noise in the absence of a masker), stress and anxiety before sleep, a disturbed circadian cortisol curve, and obstructive sleep apnea if present.
DN_S.strategyReasoning:
- Recommended carrier sound: deep brown noise, dense waterfalls, a mountain stream with a filtered pink layer.

- Recommended `recommended_noise` in the library: `brown_lowpass_500` (the deepest) or `brown_pure`.

- Recommended ratio: 0.50/0.50 (equal) to 0.30/0.70 (noise-dominant) for acute insomnia.

- Recommended duration: continuous throughout the entire 8-hour sleep window. Do not use a sleep timer — sudden silence in the middle of the night instantly triggers the amygdala at the first light transition between sleep cycles.

- Top use cases by priority: `sleep_deep` → `falling_asleep` → `relaxation` (evening winding down).

timeline.expectation: usually a slight shortening of sleep latency appears first (the time from lying down to the first sleep). The noise remains, but the contrast with silence decreases, which breaks the chain reaction "tinnitus → panic → cortisol spike".
studies record a THI improvement of about 11.8% (week 2) to 17.6% (week 4) with consistent application of sound therapy.

with consistent application, part of the deep slow-wave sleep is restored — pink/brown noise has a confirmed positive effect on SWS (stage 3) consolidation. THI drop in the range of −13 to −25 points.
with a 6-month course of bimodal + sound therapy, 73.4% of patients reach a clinically significant improvement on the TFI (≥13 point drop). The minimum therapeutic window is 9–18 months.

DN_S.additional:
- Sleep hygiene: keep a consistent bedtime and wake time ±30 minutes.

- "Waking at 3 AM" scenario: if after 15–20 minutes you haven't returned to sleep, get up gently and go to another room with continuous pink noise slightly below the loudness of the tinnitus. Do a 5-minute 4-7-8 breathing session. Return to bed when natural drowsiness appears.

- Coffee, salt, alcohol: limit caffeine after 2 PM; reduce sodium in the evening (fluid retention worsens the low frequencies of the tinnitus); avoid alcohol as a "sleep aid" — it fragments sleep.

- Do not use white noise. With prolonged nighttime listening, white noise leads to cortical dedifferentiation and paradoxical worsening — this effect is known as the "cobra effect".

- Additional techniques: modified CBT-I (cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia) is the gold standard. The classic SCT protocol requires modification — never stay in a completely silent room.
DN_S.medicalFlags:
- To an ENT: if the tinnitus is one-sided or pulsatile; if you notice dizziness, vertigo or a feeling of fullness in the ear (possible Ménière's disease).

- To a sleep specialist: if you have loud snoring, breathing pauses during the night, daytime sleepiness — it may be obstructive sleep apnea, which requires a PSG study.

- To a general practitioner: if you take diuretics, high doses of NSAIDs or antibiotics and notice worsening — some medications are ototoxic.

reasons.relaxation: Balanced nature sounds to reduce the buzzing

SS_R.description: Profile "Stress-sensitive". A detailed description will be added to this app soon.
SS_R.expectations: Detailed expectations for this profile will be added soon.
SS_R.meaning:
Your tinnitus is strongly linked to the state of your nervous system — it intensifies with stress, anxiety, conflict and emotional load, and eases when you're calm. Mild external sounds or everyday events can temporarily increase the perception of the internal noise.

This state is mediated by high lability of the autonomic nervous system and the limbic structure — the amygdala and the anterior cingulate are responsible for the over-reactive assessment of the signal as a "threat". In a typical situation, the brain of people with persistent tinnitus enters a defensive mode in which the internal noise keeps the sympathetic axis active.

In some people with this profile, reactive tinnitus is observed — the internal noise paradoxically intensifies temporarily in response to new external sounds. In that case, aggressive broadband masking is contraindicated.
SS_R.why:
This profile is often associated with prior anxiety symptoms, a perfectionist personality type, constant professional or family stress, and sometimes — with general hypersensitivity (or hyperacusis — increased sensitivity to normal sounds).

Aggravating factors: early afternoon after coffee, conflict situations, consecutive nights of insufficient sleep, news information overload, and social isolation in a quiet environment.
SS_R.strategyReasoning:
- Recommended carrier sound: extremely gentle nature blends and green noise (centered around the mid frequencies).

- Recommended `recommended_noise` in the library: green noise is not yet available in the library — as a temporary fallback we use `brown_lowpass_500` for deep calming and gentle meditation tracks (`grounded`, `inner_balance`).

- Recommended ratio: for acute anxiety → 0.30/0.70 (noise dominant) for quick suppression of the sensory contrast; for daily listening → 0.50/0.50.

- Recommended duration: use SOS mode (3–10 min noise-dominant) during acute peaks; daily background listening 2–4 hours spread throughout the day.

- Top use cases by priority: `anxiety` → `relaxation` → `meditation`.

timeline.expectation: with a properly chosen green noise or soft nature sounds, most people feel the sharpness of the situation decrease within 5–15 minutes. This is the immediate anxiolytic effect of the mid-frequency sound overture.
with consistent daily application, baseline reactivity decreases — the amplitude of the peaks in the stress response drops.
expect a reduction on the emotional subscales of the THI/TFI; full habituation and re-tuning of the appraisal process require more time.
with a combined intervention (sound + a structured CBT course) a clinically significant improvement is achieved by the majority of patients.

SS_R.additional:
- Breathing techniques: 4-7-8 breathing during acute peaks (inhale 4 sec, hold 7, exhale 8). This activates the vagus nerve and lowers sympathetic tone.

- Information hygiene: limit social media and news in the first 30 minutes after waking and the last 60 minutes before sleep.

- Caffeine: in this profile caffeine has a particularly strong effect — consider switching to 1 cup in the morning or decaf.

- Listening environment: avoid active noise-cancelling headphones — in some people with reactive tinnitus they amplify the perception of the internal noise by creating absolute silence.

- Additional techniques: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), mindfulness-based practices and a structured CBT course are particularly suitable for this profile.
SS_R.medicalFlags:
- To a psychiatrist or psychotherapist: if you have persistent anxiety, panic attacks, obsessive thoughts about the tinnitus, or symptoms of depression for more than 2 weeks.

- To an ENT: if you notice hyperacusis (normal sounds become painfully loud) — an audiological examination with tympanometry and stapedial reflex is needed.

- To a general practitioner: with frequent episodes of palpitations, sweating, dizziness, trembling — to rule out endocrine (thyroid) or cardiovascular causes that can mimic anxiety.

reasons.anxiety: SOS breathing + calming sounds during crises

SM_F.description: Profile "Somatic / Fluctuating". A detailed description will be added to this app soon.
SM_F.expectations: Detailed expectations for this profile will be added soon.
SM_F.meaning:
Your tinnitus has a variable character — its loudness changes throughout the day, sometimes significantly, and it's often linked to physical tension in the neck, jaw or shoulders. You may notice that with certain movements, head positions or clenching of the jaw the noise changes — this is the "somatic modulating" phenomenon.

This type is mediated by an interaction between the trigeminal and somatosensory systems (neck, temporomandibular joint) and the auditory pathways. Abnormal signals from tense or dysfunctional tissues (TMD — temporomandibular dysfunction, cervical dysfunction) are "rerouted" to the auditory cortex and modulate the phantom sound.

A clinical study of orofacial therapy shows that reducing pain in the temporomandibular joint explains about 35% of the drop in the TFI index in this subtype.
SM_F.why:
This profile is often associated with bruxism (clenching or grinding of teeth — at night or during the day), forward-head posture (head pushed forward, especially during long screen work), neck trauma (whiplash), or previous athletic/occupational stress on the cervical region.

Aggravating factors: long hours at the computer without breaks, carrying heavy bags on one shoulder, sleeping on the stomach, and stress-induced jaw clenching.
SM_F.strategyReasoning:
- Recommended carrier sound: highly dynamic and non-periodic natural environments — ocean tides, modulated wave swells, binaural ambient.

- Recommended `recommended_noise` in the library: `brown_lowpass_1000` for an even body of sound; mix with dynamic natural files (for example `water_wave_ocean_wave_swells`, ocean swells, breathing waves).

- Recommended ratio: 0.85/0.15 (sound dominant) — the dynamics of the natural sound engage attention and prevent cortical adaptation.

- Recommended duration: 2–4 hours a day, with emphasis on periods of physical engagement — walking, routine tasks. This combines motor distraction with sound enrichment.

- Top use cases by priority: `relaxation` → `daily` → `meditation`.

timeline.expectation: with parallel orofacial physiotherapy you may notice rapid fluctuations — good and bad days. This does not mean the process isn't working; it means the built-up tissue tension is being released.
a clinical study with a 9-week orofacial course recorded an average TFI reduction of −13.8 points, while the delayed-therapy group showed only −5.0 points. The MCID for TFI is 13 points.
with consistent application of sound + physiotherapy + limiting bruxism: average THI reductions in the range of −8.6 to −24.6 points.
in this profile long-term consistency is especially important — re-tuning the cross-modal interactions requires at least 9–18 months.

SM_F.additional:
- Posture: at the computer — screen at eye level, feet on the floor, ears aligned over the shoulders. Micro-breaks every 30–45 minutes with gentle flexion-extension movements of the neck.

- Jaw: if you notice clenching during the day — place a small reminder sticker on the computer; in the evening consider an occlusal splint after a dental check-up.

- Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR): a 10-minute evening session focused on the neck, jaw and shoulders is particularly suitable.

- Listening environment: avoid long listening with in-ear headphones — they can create TMJ strain in some people; prefer open-back or speakers.

- Additional techniques: orofacial physiotherapy and, if needed, a consultation with a dentist specializing in TMD are proven effective as a complement to sound therapy.
SM_F.medicalFlags:
- To an oral surgeon or TMD specialist: if you have pain when opening the mouth, clicking in the jaw joint, limited opening (<35 mm), or morning headaches in the temples — temporomandibular dysfunction is possible.

- To a physiotherapist (cervical specialty): if you notice neck pain, limited rotation, or a link between neck movements and the intensity of the tinnitus.

- To an ENT: if the modulation is not limited to the neck/jaw and the tinnitus becomes pulsatile, or if episodes of vertigo appear.

reasons.meditation: Guided sessions to reduce physical tension
reasons.relaxation: Dynamic nature sounds against the cycles

HB_M.description: Profile "Adapted / Mild". A detailed description will be added to this app soon.
HB_M.expectations: Detailed expectations for this profile will be added soon.
HB_M.meaning:
You have tinnitus, but it bothers you mainly in conditions of absolute silence and has no significant impact on your daily tasks, concentration, sleep or mood. Your nervous system has already built a high degree of natural habituation — your brain largely filters the phantom signal as an unimportant background.

In the clinical model this state reflects preserved inhibitory control in the central auditory pathways and adequate functioning of the limbic appraisal structures — they do not assign a "threat" to the signal.

The goal of sound therapy in this profile is not active suppression of the tinnitus (since it does not burden you), but prevention — maintaining a light sound background that prevents an increase in the sensory contrast in moments of absolute silence.
HB_M.why:
The adapted profile is often associated with a later or less traumatic onset of the tinnitus, with a stable personality structure without a tendency toward anxiety, with an active and social lifestyle, or with previous successful individual experience coping with a similar stimulus.

Aggravating factors that can temporarily destabilize HB_M: a severe untreated period of stress, prolonged illness, a sleepless night, acute sound exposure (a concert without protection), or a new medical problem (an ototoxic medication).
HB_M.strategyReasoning:
- Recommended carrier sound: ultra-soft nature sounds — rustling leaves, quiet summer rain, light wind, rain behind the window.

- Recommended `recommended_noise` in the library: `brown_lowpass_500` (minimal, only for background enrichment) or `none` — often just the natural sound without additional noise is enough.

- Recommended ratio: 0.85/0.15 or even 0.95/0.05 — the sound should be barely perceptible.

- Recommended duration: 30–60 minutes in moments of absolute silence (evening before sleep, early morning, prolonged work in a quiet room).

- Top use cases by priority: `relaxation` → `falling_asleep` → `daily`.

timeline.expectation: in the HB_M profile usually don't expect dramatic changes in perception, because the baseline state is already good. The goal is maintenance, not improvement.
expect quieter evenings in silence to become more pleasant. Emotional neutrality toward the tinnitus is preserved or slightly strengthened.
prophylactic sound enrichment reduces the likelihood of destabilization episodes during future stress.

if you notice the tinnitus suddenly becomes more intrusive for more than 2 weeks, re-evaluate your profile through the quiz — a transition to another profile is possible (for example DN_S with a new sleep problem or SS_R with new stress).

HB_M.additional:
- Maintain what works: an active lifestyle, a social environment, physical activity, good sleep. These factors are the strongest protection against progression.

- Hearing protection: at concerts, tennis tournaments, with firearms or loud sounds in the work environment — wear protective earplugs. One acute exposure can destabilize the profile for months.

- Sound environment: avoid long listening to music at high levels on headphones — for tinnitus the general recommendation is <60% of max level for <60 minutes.

- Behavioural support: an active realistic assessment — don't turn the tinnitus into a "problem" that doesn't yet exist. This preserves the positive emotional appraisal of the limbic system.

- Additional techniques: active CBT/ACT interventions are not necessary in this profile, but good general sleep hygiene and stress management remain useful.
HB_M.medicalFlags:
- With a change in character: if the tinnitus suddenly becomes one-sided, pulsatile, or accompanied by hearing loss — immediately to an ENT.

- With prolonged destabilization: if the baseline state worsens and stays worsened for >2 weeks — re-evaluate through the quiz and seek a consultation (audiologist or ENT).

- Preventively: one baseline audiological examination (audiogram) is recommended if you haven't had one — it provides a baseline for future comparison if something changes.

sleep_deep.title: Deep sleep
sleep_deep.subtitle: All night
sleep_deep.definition: A sound mode for nighttime use during natural sleep, aimed at reducing the acoustic contrast between the phantom tinnitus signal and the quiet bedroom, lowering the reactivity of the amygdala at transitions between sleep cycles, and protecting REM/N3 architecture. It does not aim for full masking, but for blending with the tinnitus at Jastreboff's "mixing point".

whenUsed.timeOfDay: Start 5–10 min before falling asleep, continues throughout the night.
whenUsed.context: A quiet dark bedroom; temperature 15.5–20°C; no screens or blue light 1 hour before sleep.
whenUsed.duration: Recommended continuous throughout the 8-hour sleep window. The user can set a sleep timer (30/60/90/120 min) in settings if they prefer automatic shut-off.

whenNotUsed: With comorbid obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) without consulting a doctor — the recurrent desaturations increase sympathetic activation.
With epilepsy or severe psychiatric disorders, if the session includes binaural beats.
With acute ear pain or unexplained one-sided tinnitus without an examination.

recommendedNoise.description: Pink noise with a fractal character, spectral slope −3 dB/octave. It matches the logarithmic arrangement of the hair cells in the cochlea; the waveform simulates natural environments (waterfall, rain, wind). For hyperacusis: low-pass above 5 kHz, or switch to brown noise (−6 dB/octave).

mixRatio.reasoning: Layer 1 (main sound — nature/melody): 30–40%. Layer 2 (background pink or brown noise): 60–70%. Research gives absolute parameters (pink noise 2–3 dB below the tinnitus threshold; total 45–55 dBA for adults, <50 dBA for children), but not a percentage X/Y. The dominance of Layer 2 follows from the logic that the background mask is the therapeutically active element.

sleep_deep.expectedEffect: Reduced reactivity of the amygdala at nighttime transitions between sleep cycles; protection of REM and N3 (deep slow-wave) sleep; with prolonged use — stimulation of neuroplasticity and gradual cognitive habituation to the tinnitus. Therapeutic goal: weekly sleep efficiency (SE) ≥85%.
safety: Maximum 50 dBA at night (for children strictly <50 dBA).
Levels above 50 dBA begin subclinical hearing damage and disrupt sleep homeostasis.
Systematic nighttime exposure >50 dB is associated with a risk of dementia (cortical atrophy, tau proteins).
No white noise, no peaks >8 kHz, no abrupt transients.
Loudness standard: −23 LUFS integrated, true peak ≤ −1 dBTP.
Wellness disclaimer mandatory — the product is not a medical device.

faq.q: How loud should I play the sound at night?
faq.a: Between 45 and 55 dBA for adults; strictly below 50 dBA for children and infants. The pink noise should be set 2–3 dB below the threshold of your subjective tinnitus — the two sounds should blend partially (Jastreboff's mixing point), not mask it completely.

faq.q: How long can I play it during the night? All night?
faq.a: Recommended continuous throughout the 8-hour sleep window. In the scientific literature there are two conflicting views: some studies indicate that shut-off timers after 30–60 min are a "serious therapeutic mistake", because sudden silence triggers the amygdala at transitions between sleep cycles; others recommend a 30–40 min or 60–90 min timer to protect REM. In AURALIS: the default is continuous; the user can set a sleep timer (30/60/90/120 min) in settings if they prefer automatic shut-off.

faq.q: Can I listen to it with noise-blocking earplugs or in-ear headphones?
faq.a: We don't recommend long-term blocking of the ear canal with standard in-ear "earbud"-type headphones — it causes an occlusion effect that makes the internal tinnitus louder, hinders the natural migration of earwax and creates conditions for bacterial infections (external otitis). We recommend open speakers in the bedroom or high-quality bone-conduction headphones, which leave the ear canal free.

faq.q: When will I feel an effect?
faq.a: A first therapeutic response is possible as early as the first 2 weeks (subacute interval 11–31 days) — a drop of ≥10 points on the THI scale. A real clinically significant effect from sound therapy with a filtered frequency notch: −8.6 THI points after 3 months, −24.6 points after 6 months. In a large 9-month RCT the median THI drops from 50 to 35. Don't expect the tinnitus to disappear completely — the goal is habituation (the brain stops treating it as a threat), not removal.

faq.q: Is long listening every night dangerous?
faq.a: It's dangerous only if the level exceeds 50 dBA — above that threshold subclinical hearing damage begins, sleep homeostasis is disrupted, and with chronic nighttime exposure above 50 dB a long-term risk is documented (cortical atrophy, tau proteins, accelerated cognitive aging). With 45–55 dBA at night (for children <50 dBA), the 8-hour cycle is safe long-term. The WHO/NIOSH lifetime safe limit is 70 dBA for 24/7 — the nighttime recommendation is well below this threshold.

faq.q: Can I listen while taking sleep medication or antidepressants?
faq.a: Sound therapy does not interact pharmacologically with medications. But note: some classes of antidepressants (as well as high-dose aspirin, ibuprofen, certain antibiotics, diuretics) can affect tinnitus on their own — their plasma levels fluctuate during the day. If you notice your tinnitus worsening with a particular medication, consult your doctor. AURALIS is a wellness app, it does not replace medical advice.

faq.q: What if it doesn't work after a few weeks?
faq.a: First: check that you're following the rules — 45–55 dBA, continuous background (not a shut-off timer if you're on the default mode), no white noise, the night in a dark bedroom without screens. Second: add a daytime CBT-I/ACT practice (the meditation category) — sound therapy is more effective combined with a cognitive-behavioural element. Third: if after 6 months there's no change in the THI score — we recommend a consultation with an audiologist or tinnitus specialist. AURALIS does not cure tinnitus, but helps with habituation.

falling_asleep.title: Falling asleep
falling_asleep.subtitle: 30–90 min before sleep
falling_asleep.definition: A mode for the last 30–90 minutes before bed, aimed at a smooth reduction of sympathetic activation, activation of the parasympathetic nervous system and shortening of sleep onset latency (SOL). It builds a conditioned association between a specific sound and muscle relaxation, which subsequently supports faster falling asleep.

whenUsed.timeOfDay: The last 30–90 min before your set bedtime.
whenUsed.context: Bed or a comfortable armchair; dim warm light without blue wavelengths; no screens.
whenUsed.duration: 15–30 min of an active relaxation session (for example progressive muscle relaxation with a background sound). After this session you can switch to "Deep sleep" mode.

whenNotUsed: Right after a heavy meal or intake of caffeine/alcohol (caffeine is stopped after noon, alcohol — in the evening).
During an acute panic attack — there, first apply "Anxiety" mode.
In bed during the day — the bed should stay associated with "sleep + relaxation", not with daytime activity.

recommendedNoise.description: The same fractal pink noise used at night — to build a conditioned association sound → relaxation. During a 15-minute progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) the "same fractal pink noise" is played. Alternatively: fractal natural tones (waterfall, light rain) at a low level.

mixRatio.reasoning: Layer 1 (leading relaxation — breathing exercise, PMR voice guide, or melody): 40–50%. Layer 2 (background pink noise): 50–60%. Layer 1 carries slightly more weight than in "Deep sleep" mode, because the relaxation content is actively focused; Layer 2 is still dominant.

falling_asleep.expectedEffect: Shortening of sleep onset latency (SOL); activation of vagal tone; lowering of heart rate and blood pressure; a smooth transition to the N1/N2 phases of sleep. With prolonged use — building a stable conditioned link "sound = relaxation".
safety: The same limits as "Deep sleep" — maximum 50 dBA.
No white noise, no abrupt transients.
If the session doesn't lead to drowsiness after 20 min, don't force it — get up and switch to a quiet activity in another room with light background pink noise until natural somnolence appears.
Loudness standard: −23 LUFS integrated, true peak ≤ −1 dBTP.
Wellness disclaimer mandatory.

faq.q: How loud?
faq.a: During the 15–30 min active relaxation session before sleep, the background sound should be similar to or slightly higher than the planned nighttime level (45–55 dBA for adults). For children below 50 dBA. The same recommendation as for "Deep sleep" mode — mixing point, not full masking.

faq.q: How many minutes before I fall asleep?
faq.a: 30–90 min before your set bedtime. The active relaxation session (for example progressive muscle relaxation with fractal pink noise) is 15–30 min. After it — switch to "Deep sleep" mode, don't turn off the sound.

faq.q: Which is better — headphones or a speaker?
faq.a: Open speakers in the bedroom or bone-conduction headphones. Standard in-ear "earbuds" are not recommended — the occlusion effect makes the tinnitus louder; a risk of earwax and infections with long use.

faq.q: What if I don't fall asleep after 20 min?
faq.a: Don't force it. Get up and move to an adjacent room (living room) with a light background pink noise 3–5 dB quieter than the nighttime level and dim warm light. Read a paper book, drink a glass of warm water or eat a light carbohydrate snack. Return to bed only when you feel real somnolence (heavy eyelids, repeated yawning). Don't look at a clock or phone — light and awareness of the time activate the HPA axis and block falling asleep.

faq.q: Can I do it with a breathing exercise?
faq.a: Yes, highly recommended. The 4-7-8 technique (inhale 4 sec, hold 7 sec, exhale 8 sec) activates the parasympathetic fibers of the vagus nerve, slows the heart rate and suppresses the amygdala. Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) for 15 min with background pink noise is also possible — it builds a conditioned association sound → relaxation.

faq.q: When will I feel the effect of shortened sleep latency?
faq.a: With systematic daily application — usually within the first 1–2 weeks, together with the other components of the therapy. The exact reduction in latency is individual; track it with a sleep journal (bedtime, time to fall asleep, nighttime awakenings). The goal is weekly sleep efficiency (SE) ≥85%.

faq.q: What if the mode wakes me up instead of putting me to sleep?
faq.a: The sound is too loud — lower it by 3–5 dB. Or the content is too stimulating — replace music with voice, nature, or fractal pink noise alone (without Layer 1). Avoid new or experimental sounds before sleep — the brain needs a familiar, stable acoustic signature.

relaxation.title: Relaxation
relaxation.subtitle: Before sleep, reading
relaxation.definition: A mode for passive relaxation during the day or evening — reading a paper book, light conversation, a glass of warm water or tea, low-energy activities. The goal is a general lowering of psychophysiological tone, not preparation for sleep. It can also be used during a nighttime awakening when temporarily leaving the bed (Night Wake Protocol).

whenUsed.timeOfDay: Afternoon or early evening; also as part of the Night Wake Protocol after a nighttime awakening.
whenUsed.context: Living room (NOT bed), dim warm light, a comfortable position.
whenUsed.duration: 20–60 min, flexible according to need. 60 min is a conservative upper limit relative to the general 60/60 rule.

whenNotUsed: As a substitute for sleep (for that use "Deep sleep" mode).
In the bed itself during the day — the bed should stay associated with "sleep + relaxation", not with daytime activity.
While driving or operating machinery — the mode aims to lower heart rate and conscious activity.

recommendedNoise.description: A soft, continuous neutral sound — pink noise or fractal natural tones, tuned below the subjective mixing point, to reduce the acoustic contrast without drawing attention. For nighttime use (Night Wake) — the same pink noise, but 3–5 dB quieter than the daytime level.

mixRatio.reasoning: Layer 1 (main sound — nature, light instrumental music): 40–50%. Layer 2 (background pink noise): 50–60%. Similar to "Falling asleep" — Layer 1 can be more varied (music, nature), Layer 2 stays dominant for the masking function.

relaxation.expectedEffect: Lowering of psychophysiological tone; reduction of heart rate; parasympathetic dominance. For nighttime use (Night Wake) — easing the return to sleep without triggering the conditioned link "bed = anxiety".
safety: Maximum 70 dBA for unlimited time (WHO/NIOSH lifetime safe).
60/60 rule (60% volume / 60 min) with 10–15 min of complete silence afterward.
No white noise.
Wellness disclaimer mandatory.

faq.q: How loud?
faq.a: Below the mixing point of the tinnitus — a soft, continuous neutral sound. For daytime relaxation: 50–65 dBA total in the room. For Night Wake (nighttime relaxation in an adjacent room): 3–5 dB quieter than the daytime level.

faq.q: How long can I play it?
faq.a: 20–60 min is a typical session. For longer listening follow the 60/60 rule (60% volume / 60 min), followed by 10–15 min of complete silence to protect against auditory fatigue. At levels below 70 dBA there is no daily limit (WHO/NIOSH lifetime safe).

faq.q: Can I do it in bed?
faq.a: We don't recommend daytime relaxation in bed — the bed should stay associated with "sleep", not with daytime relaxation. This is a key rule for people with tinnitus-induced insomnia. For daytime relaxation: a living room, a comfortable armchair, not the bed.

faq.q: Is it suitable for anxiety?
faq.a: Relaxation is not a substitute for "Anxiety" mode (SOS). During an acute panic attack — first "Anxiety" mode (4-7-8 breathing, anchor phrase, familiar pink noise 3–5 dB quieter). After stabilization → relaxation can support parasympathetic dominance. Relaxation ≠ crisis.

faq.q: Earplugs or a speaker?
faq.a: The same recommendation as "Deep sleep" — open speakers or bone-conduction headphones; not in-ear "earbuds" for long sessions.

faq.q: Is it dangerous if I fall asleep with a relaxation session?
faq.a: No — but if you regularly fall asleep with relaxation, switch to "Deep sleep" mode instead. "Deep sleep" is specially tuned for an 8-hour sleep and a mixing point quieter than relaxation. The daytime relaxation level, maintained all night, can fragment sleep.

daily.title: Daily
daily.subtitle: Work, concentration
daily.definition: A mode for daytime use during work or concentration, aimed at providing a light, masking acoustic enrichment of the work environment that keeps the tinnitus out of the focus of conscious attention and stimulates cortical habituation. It simulates the natural masking provided by ecological and industrial sound sources during the day.

whenUsed.timeOfDay: Working hours, before 12:00 ideally; in the afternoon for concentration tasks.
whenUsed.context: Office, home office, reading, studying — seated activity with focus.
whenUsed.duration: 1–2 hours during waking sessions; can be longer at low levels (below 70 dBA).

whenNotUsed: While driving or operating heavy machinery — it distracts attention; high cognitive consequences of an error.
During meetings, calls, conversations — the lead sound interferes with communication.
During an acute panic attack — use "Anxiety" mode.

recommendedNoise.description: A light, masking pink noise — the brain needs a familiar, stable acoustic signature it has already begun to habituate to. The daytime level is 3–5 dB higher than the nighttime one (due to the higher daytime hearing threshold). Alternatively: fractal natural tones (wind, distant rain).

mixRatio.reasoning: Layer 1 (optional, soft main sound — nature, instrumental music without vocals): 20–30%. Layer 2 (background pink noise): 70–80%. The daytime mode is more background than "Deep sleep" — Layer 1 is minimal or absent; Layer 2 dominates, because the goal is acoustic enrichment, not active listening. Music with vocals is excluded, because vocals compete for cognitive resources for work.

daily.expectedEffect: Keeping the tinnitus out of the focus of conscious attention through partial ecological masking; stimulating cortical habituation during the day; reducing the daytime somatic fatigue caused by tinnitus-induced insomnia.
safety: 70 dBA maximum for unlimited time (WHO/NIOSH lifetime safe).
80 dBA = maximum 40 hours per week.
60/60 rule (60% device volume, no more than 60 min, then 10–15 min of silence).
Note: emotionally liked sounds are subjectively perceived as quieter than they physically are — use an objective SPL meter (or a dB measurement app) when adjusting.
For children — strictly below 50 dBA.
Wellness disclaimer mandatory.

faq.q: How loud during work?
faq.a: Below 70 dBA — this is the WHO/NIOSH lifetime safe limit for 24/7 exposure. In practice: 50–65 dBA in the work environment is enough for a masking effect without interfering with concentration. For children — strictly below 50 dBA.

faq.q: How long per day?
faq.a: 1–2 hours actively during waking sessions — indicated in research for pink noise. At levels below 70 dBA it can be longer. Follow the 60/60 rule (60% volume / 60 min of active listening), followed by 10–15 min of silence to protect against auditory fatigue.

faq.q: Can I use music with vocals?
faq.a: Layer 1 with vocals is not recommended for the daytime mode — vocals compete for cognitive resources during work. Recommended: instrumental music without vocals, natural sounds (wind, distant rain), or just fractal pink noise.

faq.q: Headphones for work?
faq.a: Bone-conduction headphones are the best choice for a long workday — they leave the ear canal free and don't cause an occlusion effect. Open on-ear headphones are also acceptable. Avoid in-ear "earbuds" for 4+ hours a day.

faq.q: Does it interfere with concentration?
faq.a: No — the goal of the daytime mode is the opposite: to keep the tinnitus out of the focus of conscious attention, freeing the prefrontal cortex for work. If you feel that Layer 1 (melody/nature) distracts you — remove it and leave only Layer 2 (pink noise).

faq.q: Caffeine and sound therapy?
faq.a: Caffeine acts as a vasoconstrictor and central stimulant — it increases anxiety and cortical sensitivity, worsening tinnitus. Sound therapy does not neutralize this effect. Recommendation: stop caffeine after 12:00. Alcohol in the evening too — it disrupts the perfusion of the inner ear.

faq.q: When will I feel an effect on daytime tinnitus?
faq.a: Short daily 5-minute sessions are more effective than one long session — research indicates that 3 short 3-minute trainings a day are more effective for neuroplastic consolidation than a single 60-minute practice per week. First signs of reduced daytime awareness of the tinnitus — usually 2–4 weeks with systematic use.

anxiety.title: Anxiety
anxiety.subtitle: SOS, panic attack
anxiety.definition: A short emergency mode for an acute tinnitus-induced anxiety crisis or panic attack — typically during a nighttime awakening at 3:00, but also during a daytime peak. The goal is immediate stabilization through a behavioural and acoustic algorithm: vagal activation, reduction of sympathetic hyperactivity and cognitive neutralization of the signal.

whenUsed.timeOfDay: In case of acute need — at night (nighttime awakening), during the day (anxiety peak).
whenUsed.context: Keep your eyes closed or look toward a dark area. Avoid the clock and the phone screen — light and awareness of the time activate the HPA axis.
whenUsed.duration: First 30 seconds — limbic stabilization; minutes 1–15 — continued breathing; if sleep is not restored after 15–20 min at night → get up and switch to "Relaxation" in an adjacent room.

whenNotUsed: For long-term daily listening — the mode is SOS, not daily therapy.
As a substitute for psychiatric help in severe or prolonged panic attacks — wellness disclaimer.

recommendedNoise.description: The same pink noise used at night — the brain needs a familiar, stable acoustic signature. Not a new or experimental sound. Re-tuned (hardware/software) 3–5 dB quieter than the standard daytime level.

mixRatio.reasoning: Layer 1 (voice guidance — 4-7-8 breathing voice guide, anchor phrase): 50–60%. Layer 2 (background pink noise): 40–50%. Layer 1 (the guiding voice) is more dominant, because structured breathing is the active therapeutic element, while the background noise is supportive.

anxiety.expectedEffect: Stimulation of the parasympathetic fibers of the vagus nerve; forced slowing of the heart rate; lowering of blood pressure; gradual suppression of amygdala hyperactivity; interruption of the adrenaline/cortisol spike. 4-7-8 technique: inhale through the nose for 4 seconds, hold the breath for 7 seconds, exhale through the mouth for 8 seconds.
safety: Do NOT increase the volume during panic — "panic masking" (increasing it to fully drown out the tinnitus) trains the amygdala to perceive the tinnitus as a critical threat, worsening the long-term prognosis.
Stay at the normal nighttime/daytime level (3–5 dB below the tinnitus threshold).
Wellness disclaimer mandatory — the product does not replace psychiatric care in severe or recurring panic attacks.

faq.q: What should I do in the first 30 seconds during a panic attack?
faq.a: Three steps: (1) Visual block — close your eyes or look toward a dark area. Do NOT look at a clock or screen (light and awareness of the time activate the HPA axis). (2) 4-7-8 breathing — inhale through the nose for 4 seconds, hold 7 seconds, exhale through the mouth for 8 seconds. It stimulates the vagus nerve. (3) Cognitive anchor phrase — repeat in your mind: "This is just harmless electrical activity in my auditory nerve. My body is safe and will now relax."

faq.q: How loud should I play the sound during a panic attack?
faq.a: The same normal level as the nighttime mode — 3–5 dB quieter than the daytime level of the tinnitus. Do NOT increase the volume to drown it out — this is "panic masking" and trains the amygdala to perceive the tinnitus as a critical threat, worsening the long-term prognosis.

faq.q: How often can I use it?
faq.a: "Anxiety" mode is SOS — for acute crises, not for daily use. If you use the protocol more than 2–3 times a week, this is a signal to consult a doctor or psychotherapist. AURALIS does not replace psychiatric care. For a daily supportive effect use "Daily" and "Meditation".

faq.q: What if the breathing doesn't help?
faq.a: If after 15–20 min of 4-7-8 breathing and the anchor phrase stabilization doesn't occur (at night — falling back asleep), get up and move to an adjacent room (living room) with a light background pink noise. Don't stay in bed in a state of anxiety — prolonged tossing builds the conditioned link "bed = anxiety + noise". Return to bed only with real somnolence.

faq.q: The sound scares me more, it doesn't help. Why?
faq.a: Possible reasons: (1) The volume is too high — lower it to 3–5 dB below the tinnitus threshold. (2) The sound is new or unfamiliar — research recommends a familiar acoustic signature (the same pink noise you use at night); not a new experimental sound at a moment of crisis. (3) The content is too stimulating — replace the music with voice (4-7-8 voice guide) or just pink noise.

faq.q: Can I use it for daytime anxiety (not at night)?
faq.a: Yes, the mode works during the day too. 4-7-8 breathing is valid always. The daytime version is shorter — usually 2–5 min to stabilization. After that you can continue with "Daily" or "Relaxation" for a supportive effect.

faq.q: When should I seek professional help?
faq.a: Immediately if: panic attacks occur more than 2–3 times a week; suicidal thoughts appear; the tinnitus is newly appeared in one ear without explanation; you have acute ear pain or hearing loss along with the tinnitus. AURALIS is a wellness app, not a medical device. For severe symptoms — a psychiatrist, audiologist or ENT specialist.

meditation.title: Meditation
meditation.subtitle: Guided sessions
meditation.definition: A mode for active, guided practice — short 5–10 min daily exercises based on the principles of CBT-I and ACT (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy): externalization of attention, cognitive defusion, body scan, radical acceptance. It is not passive listening — it requires conscious engagement.

whenUsed.timeOfDay: 3 short 3-minute sessions during the day are recommended instead of one long 60-minute one — neuroplastic consolidation through a high frequency of activation, not length.
whenUsed.context: A comfortable quiet position; not during work, driving or focused activity.
whenUsed.duration: 5–10 min per session (micro-practices); for binaural-audio sessions — 15–30 min, 1–2 times a day.

whenNotUsed: During an acute panic attack — first "Anxiety" mode, then meditation after stabilization.
Binaural beat sessions — absolutely contraindicated with epilepsy or severe psychiatric disorders.
Without quality stereo headphones for binaural — with a mono source the binaural effect disappears completely.
With severe non-adherence (3+ days of missed sessions) — the system applies Adaptive Micro-Dosing (2-minute micro-doses), it does not punish the patient.

recommendedNoise.description: Strongly depends on the session type. For body scan / defusion / acceptance: fractal pink noise at a low level or fractal natural tones (for association with relaxation). For binaural beats (alpha 8 Hz): a narrow-band carrier with a binaural beat — stereo headphones mandatory, without a broadband background noise that would mask the binaural effect.

mixRatio.reasoning: Layer 1 (guiding voice / binaural beat / guided meditation): 60–70%. Layer 2 (background pink noise or nature): 30–40%. Layer 1 (the guiding element) is dominant, because the active content (voice, binaural beat) is the therapeutically active one — the opposite of "Deep sleep", where the background is active.

meditation.expectedEffect: CBT-I/ACT practices: eliminating the emotional resistance to the tinnitus (which sustains thalamo-cortical activity); reducing hyperfocus on the auditory analyzer. Binaural beats (alpha 8 Hz): statistically lower THI score (Δ=17.33 for TMNMT+BBS vs Δ=9.00 for TMNMT alone in a 2025 RCT); a greater effect on depressive symptoms. ACT as therapy: 54.5% of patients maintain lasting improvement at 6-month follow-up (vs 20% with TRT); average THI reduction of 17.67 points (meta-analysis).
safety: Binaural beats can cause a short-term increase in irritability, anxiety or headache with an unsuitable frequency difference — lower the frequency or end the session.
Binaural beats — absolutely contraindicated with epilepsy or severe psychiatric disorders.
For binaural — stereo headphones mandatory.
Micro-practices: avoid one-hour sessions — prolonged focus on an exercise can paradoxically increase the somatic focus on the tinnitus and intensify thalamo-cortical arousal (exposure fatigue).
Wellness disclaimer mandatory.

faq.q: How long should I meditate?
faq.a: 5–10 min per session is optimal for CBT-I/ACT micro-practices. Research shows that 3 short 3-minute trainings during the day are more effective than one 60-minute session per week — for neuroplastic consolidation the frequency of activation is critical, not the length of the single session. Longer sessions can paradoxically increase the somatic focus on the tinnitus (exposure fatigue). For binaural-audio sessions: 15–30 min, 1–2 times a day.

faq.q: Are special headphones needed?
faq.a: For binaural beats — YES, high-quality stereo headphones are mandatory. With a mono source the binaural effect disappears completely. For CBT-I/ACT voice meditations (body scan, defusion, acceptance) — open speakers or bone-conduction headphones also work.

faq.q: When is binaural meditation dangerous?
faq.a: Absolutely contraindicated with epilepsy or severe psychiatric disorders. With an unsuitable frequency difference it can cause: a short-term increase in irritability, anxiety, headache. If you feel any of these → stop and switch to another type of meditation (body scan, defusion). AURALIS is a wellness app, not a medical one.

faq.q: When will I feel an effect?
faq.a: Varies by session type. CBT-I/ACT micro-practices (5-10 min daily): first therapeutic response in the subacute interval (11–31 days) — a THI drop of ≥10 points with systematic application. Binaural beats 8 Hz + probe therapy: a statistically greater effect than the probe alone in a 2025 RCT (Δ=17.33 THI points for the combined group vs Δ=9.00 for TMNMT alone). ACT as a complex therapy over 6 months: 54.5% of patients maintain lasting improvement (vs 20% with TRT); meta-analysis — average THI reduction of 17.67 points.

faq.q: What if I miss a few days?
faq.a: Don't panic. Adaptive Micro-Dosing — continue with shortened 2-minute micro-practices (for example 3 deep breaths or just relaxing the jaw). The system doesn't punish you for missed days — the goal is maintaining the habit, not perfect adherence. After 4+ days without a session — focus only on the passive sound library ("Deep sleep", "Daily") until you feel ready to resume the active practices.

faq.q: Can I do it with medications?
faq.a: Meditation has no pharmacological interaction. But when taking antidepressants, benzodiazepines or sedatives — consult a doctor for the optimal combination. ACT has been proven effective even in patients who did not respond to 6+ months of TRT therapy — meditative practices complement, not replace, medical treatment.

faq.q: Which techniques are the most important?
faq.a: According to research, the core ACT/CBT-I components for tinnitus are: (1) Radical acceptance — stopping the fight with the noise; "I allow you to be here in this moment". (2) Cognitive defusion — restructuring the metaphors ("ringing" → "wind in the branches"). (3) Body scan — reducing hyperfocus on the auditory analyzer. (4) Externalization of attention — redirecting from internal to external stimuli. (5) Reduction of avoidance behaviour — doing activities you avoided for fear of intensifying the tinnitus.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.noResults: No questions found for this word.
faq.supportHint: Didn't find an answer? Contact support by email.

items.q: Is it dangerous to listen to sounds for a long time every day?
items.a: Not dangerous at the right levels. The WHO and NIOSH set 70 dBA as the limit for unlimited time per day — that's louder than quiet conversational speech. AURALIS recommends 50–65 dBA for daytime use and 45–55 dBA at night. There's danger only if you exceed these levels — above 80 dBA there's a limit of 40 hours per week. Follow the 60/60 rule — 60% of device volume, no more than 60 minutes of active listening, followed by 10–15 minutes of silence. With these parameters, a whole life of daily listening is safe.

items.q: Can I use the app on a plane?
items.a: Yes, completely. AURALIS works entirely without internet — all sounds, questionnaires and CBT texts are local on your phone. Enable Airplane mode and the app will function as usual. This is also convenient for saving battery during a long flight. On board a plane: the background noise is usually around 75–85 dB — we recommend listening with bone-conduction headphones or high-quality open headphones, not with in-ear "earbuds". They create an occlusion effect that makes the tinnitus louder.

items.q: Should I listen with headphones or a speaker?
items.a: Prefer a speaker or bone-conduction headphones. Standard in-ear "earbuds" are not recommended for long use — they block the ear canal and create an occlusion effect that actually makes the internal tinnitus louder. Also, with long daily hours of listening the risk of earwax blockage and external otitis increases. For nighttime listening: open speakers in the bedroom are the best choice. For work or walking: bone-conduction headphones leave the ear canal free and don't cause occlusion. On-ear button headphones are also acceptable.

items.q: What's the maximum listening time per day?
items.a: There's no strict limit at the right levels. At 50–65 dBA there's no daily limit — the WHO sets 70 dBA as safe for 24/7. In practice: 4–6 hours spread throughout the day is therapeutically optimal, plus 8 hours for continuous nighttime use. Variety for the ears matters — don't keep the same headphones or angle for hours. Take short breaks every hour (the 60/60 rule). At signs of auditory fatigue — ringing, sound sensitivity, dullness — stop for 2–3 hours and continue at a lower level.

items.q: What should I do if I feel worse after listening?
items.a: Stop first, don't panic. Mild temporary worsening after listening is possible for three reasons: too high a level (lower it by 5–10 dB in the next session), the wrong type of sound for your profile (TH_C needs pink and filtered rain, not white noise), or somatic tension from the day (try the Day 8 muscle relaxation exercise). If the worsening lasts more than 48 hours after stopping, consult an ENT. Red flag: if the noise becomes one-sided, pulsatile or accompanied by hearing loss — seek a doctor immediately.

items.q: Why does the sound play with a delay after pressing the button?
items.a: This is a normal phenomenon in PWA apps. The browser or the Capacitor wrapper decodes the MP3 file before playing it — the delay is usually 100–500 ms. AURALIS uses caching in the Service Worker, so the second time the sound plays immediately. If the delay is more than 2 seconds, check: 1) is there enough memory on the phone, 2) close other apps running in the background, 3) restart AURALIS. On older phones (Android 8–9) the delay may be a bit longer.

items.q: Does AURALIS work without internet?
items.a: Yes, completely. AURALIS is an offline-first app — Bible v3 LAW No.6. All sounds (250 files) are downloaded to your phone at installation. All questionnaires (THI, journal), CBT texts and settings are kept in the phone's local storage. AURALIS doesn't send any data to a server — your answers and progress stay solely on your phone. This meets the strictest GDPR requirements for sensitive health data. Exception: only with a purchase through Google Play or the App Store, the payment is processed by the platform, not by us.

items.q: Why won't the app open?
items.a: Most common reasons: 1) First opening after installation — wait 10–20 seconds, because the system is preparing 100–150 MB of sound files. 2) Low device memory — free up 200 MB on the phone. 3) Old Android version (below 8.0) — the app requires Android 8.0+. 4) Slow first render — update to the latest version from Google Play. If the problem persists, try: close the app from the active-apps menu and open it again. If it still won't start — reinstall from Google Play (your data is kept in Profile → Export, if you did it).

items.q: Why does the battery drain fast?
items.a: It shouldn't. AURALIS uses an optimized AudioBufferSourceNode (not AudioWorklet), the system sample rate, and suspend on pause — Bible v3 LAW No.4. A whole night of listening (8 hours) should drain 3–8% of the battery. If you see higher consumption: 1) Turn off "Wi-Fi" and "Bluetooth" at night — they drain more than the audio playback itself. 2) Enable "Do not disturb" mode for the night. 3) Lower the screen brightness before sleep. 4) Check in Settings → Battery whether another app is running in the background. If AURALIS really drains a lot — report it to support, that's a technical bug.

items.q: What happens if the phone turns off at night?
items.a: The sound stops and the session is saved. When you start AURALIS again, the app offers to continue from where you stopped. All journal entries, THI answers and CBT progress are saved immediately in localStorage — they're not lost in a sudden shutdown. If the phone turns off due to a drained battery: before sleep, plug it into a charger or enable "Battery saver" mode. If the sound suddenly disappears in the middle of the night with the phone working — the amygdala will register it as a transition. We recommend a continuous nighttime session, not a sleep timer.

items.q: When will I feel the first effect?
items.a: Different for everyone. The first signs of a less intrusive noise can appear as early as the first 1–2 weeks — especially a sense of easier falling asleep and brief moments when you don't notice the tinnitus. A clinically significant result (a THI drop of 7 or more points) usually comes within 3 months with consistent application. A more serious effect (−24 THI points) is recorded at 6 months. Real habituation, where the brain stops treating the noise as a threat, is a process of 9 to 18 months. AURALIS does not cure tinnitus — it helps with habituation and a better quality of life.

items.q: What should I do if I feel it's getting worse?
items.a: First — don't draw conclusions from one or two days. Tinnitus has good and bad periods that don't reflect an overall trend. Review your journal entries for the last 7 days — often "worse today" is not a trend. Second step: think about external factors — stress, caffeine after 2 PM, a sleepless night, acute noise exposure. These often intensify tinnitus temporarily. If you really feel a lasting worsening (over 2 weeks), consult an ENT. Red flag: if the noise becomes one-sided, pulsatile, accompanied by dizziness or hearing loss — seek a doctor immediately.

items.q: How many hours a day should I listen?
items.a: For your TH_C profile — at least 2 hours a day for stable habituation; ideally 4–6 hours spread throughout the day. You don't need to listen continuously — short 30-minute sessions several times a day work better than one long session. During the night — continuous background sound throughout the entire 8-hour sleep (studies show that a sleep timer can trigger the amygdala at transitions between sleep cycles). This is a recommendation, not an obligation. Even 1 hour a day with the right sound works better than nothing. Listen whenever it's convenient for you.

items.q: Should I give up coffee?
items.a: Not completely, but limit it. Caffeine is a central stimulant — it increases cortical sensitivity and can intensify tinnitus. Recommendation: 1 cup in the morning (until 10:00), no coffee after 2 PM. This gives the body enough time to clear the caffeine before sleep (half-life ~5 hours). In your TH_C profile caffeine has a particularly strong effect on the high-frequency ringing. Alcohol in the evening also worsens it — it disrupts the perfusion of the inner ear and fragments sleep. Salt in the evening — it retains fluids, worsening the low frequencies. Try 2 weeks without caffeine after 2 PM and observe.

items.q: Do vitamins or supplements help?
items.a: AURALIS does not give medical recommendations about supplements. This falls outside our competence (Bible §1 — LAW No.2: NO MEDICAL ADVICE). There are scientific studies on magnesium and tinnitus — they show mixed results; don't recommend them on your own without consulting a doctor. A vitamin B12 deficiency can worsen tinnitus — a blood test will show whether you need it. Ginkgo biloba — the clinical evidence is weak. Never start supplements without consulting your doctor, especially if you take other medications. AURALIS focuses on what we've proven — sound therapy and CBT practices.

items.q: What is the THI and why am I filling it in?
items.a: The THI (Tinnitus Handicap Inventory) is a standardized 25-question questionnaire developed by Newman and colleagues in 1996. It measures not the loudness of the noise, but how much it bothers you in daily life. The score ranges from 0 (no handicap) to 100 (catastrophic). You fill it in on Day 1 for a baseline and on Day 14 for comparison — so you see concrete numerical progress. A drop of 7 or more points between the two tests is considered clinically significant. This is a public medical instrument used worldwide. The results stay solely on your phone.

items.q: Why 14 days? Why not shorter or longer?
items.a: 14 days is an optimized "subacute" interval, proven in clinical studies. Shorter (1–7 days) — too little to feel a change and for the brain to start habituating. Longer (3–6 months) — a large portion of users drop out before finishing. The classic Kalmeda program has a 9–12-month course, but 60% of patients discontinue it in the first 3 months. The AURALIS 14-day module includes the critical CBT elements: demystification, thought restructuring, relaxation and acceptance. After these 2 weeks you can continue with a maintenance mode — listening to sounds and short practices.

items.q: What should I do after Day 14?
items.a: The 14-day module doesn't end the tinnitus — it's a beginning. After Day 14 continue with the following: 1) Listen to the sound library every day (at least 2 hours). 2) Repeat the most helpful CBT exercises 2–3 times a week. 3) Fill in an evening journal without pressure — a short self-observation. 4) Reassess the THI after 30 days and 90 days (Profile → Repeat test). For real habituation, clinical studies recommend at least 9–18 months of consistent use. AURALIS stays on your phone as a long-term support tool, not for intensive treatment.

items.q: What if I miss a day?
items.a: Nothing is lost. AURALIS doesn't punish you for missed days — Bible §1 is wellness, not a disciplinary tool. You have 2 "rest days" (freeze days) available for the 14 days — the streak counter doesn't drop to 0. On the morning of the next day you'll see a quiet message that you can fill in the missed day late or simply continue to the next one. With 2 consecutive missed days — the system automatically reduces the next day's task to a 2-minute micro-dose (just 3 deep breaths). The sound library and Sleep mode remain fully available in any scenario.

items.q: Can I fill in the journal for a missed previous day?
items.a: Yes. In the daily hub you'll see a "Fill in Day X late" button for each missed day. This opens the same questions, but with a note "filled in on: <current date>" — so you honestly see when it was done. Note: with late filling, the accuracy of the day's recollection decreases, especially for sliders 1–5. We recommend filling in the journal on its own day (in the evening for the evening one, in the morning for the morning one). With more than 3 days' delay — it's better to skip it than to fill it in inaccurately. The entry stays in your export as a reference.
