day_1.title: Sensory demystification
day_1.theme: Tinnitus is a sound the brain creates on its own — it's not damage to the ear and not a warning of danger.
day_1.explanation: The noise you hear comes from the brain, not the ear. When the auditory pathway receives fewer signals from the surrounding world, the brain compensates with its own activity — just as the eyes "draw" points of light in complete darkness. This is not a tumor, not madness and not a sign of anything dangerous. It's a reaction that your brain can gradually get used to (habituate) and ignore.
exercise: Sit calmly and for 1 minute listen to your tinnitus — without judging it, without fighting it.
Then say out loud or in your mind: "This is a sound my brain produces. I am not in danger."
Write in one sentence what you felt when you stopped fighting the sound.
day_1.reflection: Which of these statements was hardest for you to accept today — that the noise comes from the brain, that it's not dangerous, or that it can fade?

day_2.title: The vicious cycle of distress
day_2.theme: The more you worry about the noise, the louder it becomes — and vice versa.
day_2.explanation: When you feel the tinnitus and immediately think "here it goes again", your body tenses, the heart starts beating faster, and your brain directs even more attention to the sound. Then the sound really is perceived as louder. This is a closed loop: noise → worry → tension → louder noise. Breaking it doesn't happen with force, but with understanding and a calm response.
exercise: Recall a moment from today when your tinnitus intensified.
Write three things: what was happening around you, what you thought immediately, how you felt in your body.
Read what you wrote and notice the connection between the three — thought, body, sound.
day_2.reflection: At what moment today was the tinnitus loudest, and what was happening to you just before that?

day_3.title: Catching automatic thoughts
day_3.theme: The first thought that comes with the noise is often frightening — and not always true.
day_3.explanation: When the tinnitus appears, thoughts flash through your mind for a second — so fast you barely notice them. For example: "This will never stop" or "It will destroy me". These automatic thoughts are not facts. They are a habit of the mind, built from fear. The first step toward change is simply to notice them and write them down, without judging them.
exercise: Carry a notebook with you or use the notes on your phone.
Each time your tinnitus intensifies, write down the exact thought that popped into your mind (in your own words).
By the evening, collect at least 3 such thoughts.
day_3.reflection: Which of the thoughts you wrote down today repeats most often in your head?

day_4.title: Personal anchor phrase
day_4.theme: A short, personally chosen phrase can stop the worry the moment it appears.
day_4.explanation: When you repeat a frightening thought, your brain learns it. The same is true for a calm thought. Choose your own phrase that brings you a sense of calm and resilience — for example "The noise flows like a river, I stand calmly on the bank" or "This sound can stay, I'll live my life". You'll use this anchor phrase every time the tinnitus intrudes.
exercise: Write 3 variants of a personal phrase of 5–8 words each.
Read them slowly out loud and choose the one that calms you the most.
Repeat it slowly 10 times, with calm breathing between repetitions.
day_4.reflection: Which phrase did you choose and what did you feel when you repeated it?

day_5.title: Turning attention outward
day_5.theme: The more you listen to the noise, the louder it becomes — so the mind will learn to turn outward.
day_5.explanation: When you're focused on the tinnitus, the brain works at maximum sensitivity precisely toward it. If you train your mind to turn toward the external senses — sight, other sounds, touch — it will learn that the world is bigger than the noise in your head. This skill is built with practice, just like a muscle.
exercise: Sit in a room you love. Name out loud 3 things you see around you.
Then 3 different sounds you hear around (not the tinnitus).
Finally 3 physical sensations on your skin (the chair under you, your clothes, the air on your arms).
day_5.reflection: Which sense — sight, hearing or touch — was easiest for you to notice today?

day_6.title: Gentle sound enrichment
day_6.theme: A light background sound reduces the contrast between the tinnitus and the silence.
day_6.explanation: In complete silence your brain amplifies the internal noise, because it has nothing to compare it to. When you add a quiet background sound — light rain, the sound of a river, soft pink noise — the noise in your head loses its exclusivity. The volume of the background sound is always just below the volume of the tinnitus. The goal is not to drown it out, but to gently "blend" it with the outside world.
exercise: Open the "Recommended" section in the app and play a sound that's pleasant to you.
Adjust the volume so that you hear it clearly, but the noise in your head stays perceptible.
Listen for 10 minutes, without doing anything else. Just observe how the two sounds exist at the same time.
day_6.reflection: What changed in your perception of the tinnitus during these 10 minutes?

day_7.title: Review of the first week
day_7.theme: One week back — what you learned about yourself.
day_7.explanation: Don't expect the noise to have disappeared. The goal of the first week is not silence, but a new understanding: that the tinnitus originates in the brain, that thoughts and the body intensify it, and that you have tools to respond. This week you've planted the seeds. Habituation comes with repetition, not in days — in months.
exercise: Flip through your notes from Days 1–6.
Write one moment of the week when the anchor phrase or an exercise helped you.
Write one thing that was hardest for you — and don't blame yourself for it.
day_7.reflection: Which day of this week was most helpful for you and why?

day_8.title: Progressive muscle relaxation
day_8.theme: When the body is tense, the noise feels louder — so you'll learn to relax it deliberately.
day_8.explanation: Stress contracts the muscles of the neck, shoulders and jaw. These muscles are connected to the auditory system — their tension is literally "heard" in your head as louder tinnitus. When you consciously tense and then relax a muscle, the body remembers the difference and starts to live in a calmer state.
exercise: Sit comfortably. Clench your fists hard for 5 seconds, then release them and notice the sensation for 15 seconds.
Repeat the same with the shoulders (raise them toward your ears, then release).
Finally with the neck (tilt your head slightly forward, then release it). Feel the weight of your head.
day_8.reflection: Which of the three muscles was the tightest when you started the exercise?

day_9.title: Jaw relaxation
day_9.theme: The jaw often clenches without you noticing — and the tinnitus intensifies.
day_9.explanation: Many people with ringing in the ears clench their teeth during the day without noticing. The jaw muscles are connected to the auditory nerve — when they're tense, the brain interprets them as a signal and intensifies the tinnitus. If you learn to feel your jaw and release it, you'll break one of the hidden causes of the noise being louder.
exercise: Place your fingertips on the muscles in front of your ears (where the jaw opens).
Make slow circular movements for 1 minute, with light pressure.
Finally, deliberately relax your lower lip and let the lower jaw hang slightly for 30 seconds.
day_9.reflection: How many times today did you catch yourself clenching your jaw?

day_10.title: Breathing to calm down
day_10.theme: A long exhale switches on the brake of the nervous system.
day_10.explanation: When you exhale longer than you inhale, the vagus nerve is activated. This is the body's natural brake — it slows the heart, relaxes the muscles and tells the brain you're safe. After a few minutes of such breathing, the sympathetic "alarm" subsides and the tinnitus moves away from the center of attention.
exercise: Sit upright, one hand on your chest, the other on your belly.
Inhale slowly through the nose for 4 seconds, so that the belly rises, not the chest.
Exhale slowly through the mouth for 6 seconds. Repeat the cycle 10 times.
day_10.reflection: How calm did you feel in your body after the 10 cycles, compared to before?

day_11.title: Thoughts like clouds
day_11.theme: A thought is not a fact — it's an event in the mind that comes and goes.
day_11.explanation: When a thought like "here it goes again, it will destroy me" appears, you don't have to believe it. You can simply notice it — as you would notice a cloud in the sky. Don't chase it, don't fight it, don't fan it. Watch it pass. This skill is called distancing and is one of the most powerful in dealing with tinnitus.
exercise: Sit calmly with your eyes slightly closed.
When a thought about the tinnitus appears, say in your mind: "I'm having the thought that…", then complete the thought.
Let the thought pass without reacting to it. Do this for 5 minutes.
day_11.reflection: Was it easier for you to watch your thoughts from the side, or to believe them as usual?

day_12.title: Three pleasant moments
day_12.theme: Your day contains more good moments than your brain notices.
day_12.explanation: When you have tinnitus, your mind is trained to notice mostly the unpleasant. This is not your weakness — it's a defensive reaction. But there's an antidote: each day make a short list of three pleasant sensory moments — a taste, a scent, a sight, a touch, a quiet sound. Over time your brain will start to seek them out on its own.
exercise: Before you fall asleep, recall the day.
Write three things you felt today that were pleasant (for example the aroma of coffee, warm light, a conversation).
Next to each, write one word for the feeling it gave you.
day_12.reflection: Which of the three moments was the most unexpected for you?

day_13.title: Sound and breathing together
day_13.theme: When calm nature and calm breathing meet, the tinnitus loses its power.
day_13.explanation: Until now you used breathing and the background sound separately. Today you'll combine them. The brain links the two experiences — the sound becomes a signal for relaxation, the relaxation strengthens the effect of the sound. Gradually you build a new, neutral association: the tinnitus is not alone in your awareness — around it there's a calm environment and a calm body.
exercise: Play a 10-minute nature sound session from the app (waves, a stream or rain).
Throughout, breathe slowly: 4 seconds in, 6 seconds out.
When the tinnitus speaks up, don't react — continue the sound and the breathing.
day_13.reflection: Was the tinnitus the same during these 10 minutes, or did something in the feeling change?

day_14.title: A look back and forward
day_14.theme: Two weeks back — what works for you and what you'll carry forward.
day_14.explanation: Two weeks are enough to feel a change in your attitude toward the noise, but not for full habituation. Habituation is a process of months, not days. What you'll take with you today is not a "cure", but a personal map of the tools that work for you. You'll use this map from now on — on good days and on hard ones.
exercise: Flip through all the notes and reflections from Days 1–13.
Write three things you'll keep doing every day (exercises, a phrase, a sound).
Fill in the second THI questionnaire — it will show how you feel now, compared to the first day.
day_14.reflection: Which of the things you learned during these 14 days was the most important for you personally?

TH_C.description: This profile involves a subjective sensation of high, whistling, tonal tinnitus that seriously affects cognitive processes and daily concentration. For it, high-frequency sounds such as filtered rain and balanced pink noise are recommended, tuned close to the mixing point, to facilitate habituation at the cortical level.

DN_S.description: A profile defined by the presence of dense, low-frequency sounds and serious problems falling asleep or maintaining sleep. For it, the leading choices are deeper acoustic environments (brown noise, dense waterfalls), which strongly mask the subjective buzzing and calm the mind in the evening, supporting a smooth and healthy transition to sleep.

SS_R.description: In this profile the perception of tinnitus is strongly linked to the activity of the autonomic nervous system and the limbic structure. Even mild sound stimuli or everyday stress intensify the sensation of noise. The acoustic response requires green noise for relief and extremely gentle nature blends to reduce cortical excitability.

SM_F.description: A profile corresponding to tinnitus with variable loudness and frequency, often manifesting in cycles or strongly linked to physical tension in the neck and jaw. Highly dynamic and non-periodic natural environments are used, such as ocean tides, which engage the auditory system without the risk of habituating to a static signal.

HB_M.description: This profile shows a high level of natural habituation. The noise is heard mainly in conditions of absolute silence and has no significant impact on quality of life. The acoustic recommendation aims solely to enrich the quiet environment with minimal nature sounds for the long-term prevention of sensory contrast.

intensity.high: Slightly below the tinnitus level; a soft timbre

preset_deep_calm.title: Why "Deep silence"?
preset_deep_calm.micro: Deep, bass sounds that help the body calm down. A universal mix — suitable for most people with tinnitus.
preset_deep_calm.full:
📊 WHAT THE SOUND IS:

A calm bass hum, similar to the deep noise of a fridge or a distant air conditioner. No high sounds, no abrupt changes.

🎯 WHO IT'S FOR:
✅ Universal — suitable for all profiles
✅ Especially good for people with high whistling tinnitus
✅ Helps with stress or anxiety
⚠ If you have low bass tinnitus — try it first for 5 minutes

💡 WHEN TO PLAY IT:
• Before sleep (30-60 minutes)
• During work for concentration
• In stressful moments during the day
• Recommended duration: 30-60 minutes

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT:

If you listen 30-60 minutes a day:

Week 1-2:
Mild relief. You may notice calmer sleep.

Week 3-4:
The tinnitus becomes "quieter" while you listen. Fewer thoughts about it.

Month 2-3:
You start to notice it less often. This is the first sign of habituation.

⚠ IMPORTANT:
• Results vary
• We do NOT promise a cure
• The tinnitus doesn't disappear, but the brain learns to ignore it

🧠 WHY IT WORKS:

Deep sounds don't activate the "alarm" part of the brain (the amygdala). This breaks the vicious cycle between tinnitus and stress. When stress decreases, the tinnitus sounds quieter.

📚 STUDY:

A meta-analysis of 14 studies (793 patients, 2024): after 6 months of regular listening, the average improvement on the THI scale is 24.6 points. The minimal significant improvement is 7 points — patients get over 3 times more.

preset_underwater.title: Why "Underwater relaxation"?
preset_underwater.micro: Sounds like underwater — very deep frequencies below 200Hz. Similar to what you hear when you're in a pool below the surface.
preset_underwater.full:
📊 WHAT THE SOUND IS:

A very deep, even noise — like being underwater in a pool. No high sounds, no waves, no transitions. Reminiscent of how babies hear sounds in the womb.

🎯 WHO IT'S FOR:
✅ People with high whistling tinnitus (like a whistle or chirping)
✅ Stress-sensitive profile
✅ Especially for nighttime hours and falling asleep
⚠ Not for people who have tinnitus precisely in the bass range

💡 WHEN TO PLAY IT:
• Before sleep (highly recommended)
• At night during insomnia
• During a panic attack or strong anxiety
• Recommended duration: up to 2 hours in a row

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT:

If you listen 60 minutes a day (in the evening):

Week 1-2:
Faster falling asleep. It may take 10-15 minutes less to fall asleep.

Week 3-4:
Fewer awakenings during the night. Deeper sleep.

Month 2-3:
Morning tinnitus feels quieter. This is a sign the brain is calming down.

⚠ IMPORTANT:
• Results vary
• We do NOT promise a cure
• The tinnitus doesn't disappear, but the brain learns to ignore it

🧠 WHY IT WORKS:

Deep underwater sounds (60-200Hz) mask the high tones of the tinnitus. In the same way that in a noisy room it's hard to hear a quiet conversation. Besides, these frequencies activate the calming nervous system (the parasympathetic).

📚 STUDY:

A 2024 study (TENT-A1/A2 trials, 200+ patients): after 12 weeks of using deep tonal sounds, 73% of patients achieve a significant improvement in quality of life (TFI scale, ≥13 points improvement).

preset_sea_shore.title: Why "Seashore"?
preset_sea_shore.micro: Distant sound of sea waves plus a deep bass background. A natural scene for relaxation.
preset_sea_shore.full:
📊 WHAT THE SOUND IS:

A calm sound of waves in the distance (about 100 meters), mixed with a deep bass background. You hear an ocean, but without strong surf. Like sitting on a resort terrace.

🎯 WHO IT'S FOR:
✅ Stress-sensitive profile (SS_R)
✅ Somatic/fluctuating profile (SM_F)
✅ People who love nature
⚠ Not for people with a fear of water or sea-related trauma

💡 WHEN TO PLAY IT:
• In the morning on waking (15-30 minutes)
• After work for relaxation
• Before sleep (at a lower volume)
• Recommended duration: 30-90 minutes

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT:

If you listen 30-60 minutes a day:

Week 1-2:
A feeling of vacation and calm. Less tension in the shoulders.

Week 3-4:
You start to associate the sound with relaxation. The body calms down even before you hear the sound.

Month 2-3:
The brain links the tinnitus with a pleasant memory (the sea), not with anxiety. This is the key to habituation.

⚠ IMPORTANT:
• Results vary
• We do NOT promise a cure
• The tinnitus doesn't disappear, but the brain learns to ignore it

🧠 WHY IT WORKS:

Natural water sounds activate brain areas linked to positive emotions. The tinnitus becomes "part of nature" instead of an "enemy". This is a known technique from Tinnitus Retraining Therapy (TRT) — scientists have used it for 30 years.

📚 STUDY:

A 2023 comparative study (Tong et al., 120 patients): personalized nature sounds show better results than traditional sound therapy, with 2 times less drop-out from treatment (16% vs 36% with white noise).
preset_sea_shore.source: Tong et al. (2023). 120 patients. See docs/research/11.

preset_forest_stream.title: Why "Underwater stream"?
preset_forest_stream.micro: Sounds from an underwater river current — mid frequencies around 1500Hz. A rich, "full" sound.
preset_forest_stream.full:
📊 WHAT THE SOUND IS:

Similar to what you hear when you're underwater in a river. You hear the water passing by stones. A full, mid-frequency sound — not too deep, not too bright.

🎯 WHO IT'S FOR:
✅ Low-frequency noise tinnitus (DN_S)
✅ Adapted/mild tinnitus (HB_M)
✅ People who use sound therapy for a long time
⚠ Not for people with tinnitus in the mid frequencies

💡 WHEN TO PLAY IT:
• During work or reading
• During concentration
• After a stressful day
• Recommended duration: 30-120 minutes

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT:

If you listen 60 minutes a day:

Week 1-2:
Improved concentration. Less distraction from the tinnitus while working.

Week 3-4:
Less "chatter" in the head. Clearer thinking.

Month 2-3:
The tinnitus stops being the center of your attention. It starts to become "background" instead of "foreground".

⚠ IMPORTANT:
• Results vary
• We do NOT promise a cure
• The tinnitus doesn't disappear, but the brain learns to ignore it

🧠 WHY IT WORKS:

Mid-frequency sounds (1000-2000Hz) perfectly "overlap" the zone where many tinnitus tones live. Besides, water sounds have natural variations that don't tire the brain the way constant white noise does.

📚 STUDY:

A study of acoustic environments (2024, 4-month follow-up): enriched nature sounds show 84% better treatment adherence from patients, compared to traditional white noise.
preset_forest_stream.source: EAEGT vs WN study, 2024. See docs/research/11.

preset_pink_pure.title: Why "Pink noise"?
preset_pink_pure.micro: A classic base sound for tinnitus relief. An even "rain" sound, used in clinical studies for over 30 years.
preset_pink_pure.full:
📊 WHAT THE SOUND IS:

Similar to even light rain on leaves. A constant, "fluffy" sound without high or low peaks. Scientists call it "pink" because it has more low than high frequencies (like pink light).

🎯 WHO IT'S FOR:
✅ Universal — works for all profiles
✅ A classic choice for tinnitus relief
✅ Good for people who want to start with a proven option
⚠ Sometimes it may seem monotonous — that's why we combine it with other sounds

💡 WHEN TO PLAY IT:
• When you want something simple and predictable
• As a background during sleep
• When other mixes don't work for you
• Recommended duration: 30 minutes - all night

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT:

If you listen 60 minutes a day:

Week 1-2:
The tinnitus is less perceptible while you listen. You may notice better falling asleep.

Week 3-4:
Less thinking about the tinnitus during the day. Less stress.

Month 2-3:
You start listening to the pink noise out of habit. The brain associates it with relaxation.

⚠ IMPORTANT:
• Results vary
• We do NOT promise a cure
• The tinnitus doesn't disappear, but the brain learns to ignore it

🧠 WHY IT WORKS:

Pink noise contains all frequencies at once, balanced. This gives the brain an "even menu" of sounds that masks the tinnitus better than silence. It doesn't activate the stress system, because there are no abrupt changes.

📚 STUDY:

Pink noise has been studied in dozens of RCTs since the 90s. In clinical practice (TRT therapy) it's used as a base for over 100,000 patients with significant results in sleep quality.
preset_pink_pure.source: Tinnitus Retraining Therapy (TRT) clinical practice, 30+ years. See docs/research/02.

preset_brown_pure.title: Why "Brown noise"?
preset_brown_pure.micro: Deeper than pink — it sounds like heavy rain or a distant waterfall. Ideal for falling asleep.
preset_brown_pure.full:
📊 WHAT THE SOUND IS:

Bassier than pink. Similar to heavy rain on a roof or a distant waterfall. Deep and enveloping — many people find it cozy.

🎯 WHO IT'S FOR:
✅ For falling asleep — especially good
✅ For people with high whistling tinnitus
✅ With anxiety
⚠ Not for people with low bass tinnitus (you risk intensifying it)

💡 WHEN TO PLAY IT:
• Before sleep (highly recommended)
• At night as a background
• During a panic attack
• Recommended duration: all night is safe

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT:

If you listen 8 hours during the night:

Week 1-2:
Fewer awakenings. You feel more rested in the morning.

Week 3-4:
A more stable sleep rhythm. Less "tinnitus" sound on waking.

Month 2-3:
Significantly better sleep quality. This is a known indirect improvement — good sleep reduces tinnitus.

⚠ IMPORTANT:
• Results vary
• We do NOT promise a cure
• The tinnitus doesn't disappear, but the brain learns to ignore it

🧠 WHY IT WORKS:

Brown noise has the most bass frequencies. This activates the calming nervous system (the parasympathetic) even while you sleep. Besides, it masks small noises in the house (fireplace, air conditioner, creaks) that could wake you up.

📚 STUDY:

Deep noise maskers are the basis of TRT therapy. Clinical data from 2024 show: sleep quality is the strongest predictor of tinnitus relief — patients with 7+ hours of sleep improve 2 times faster on the THI scale.
preset_brown_pure.source: TRT clinical practice + Sleep research 2024. See docs/research/09 + docs/research/11.

why_no_white_noise.title: Why don't we have white noise?
why_no_white_noise.micro: White noise is bad for tinnitus. It makes it louder, not quieter. This is scientifically proven.
why_no_white_noise.full:
📊 WHAT WHITE NOISE IS:

A "gray" sound, similar to an old TV without a signal. It contains all frequencies evenly, including the high ones. Often advertised as a "sleep aid".

🚨 WHY WE DON'T USE IT:

White noise activates the brain's anxiety structure (the amygdala). In people with tinnitus this creates the so-called "cobra effect" — when the white noise stops, the tinnitus sounds MUCH louder than before.

📈 WHAT HAPPENS IF YOU USE IT:

First days: It seems to help (masks the tinnitus)
Week 1-2: Adaptation — white noise helps less and less
Week 3-4: The tinnitus becomes LOUDER in moments when you're not listening
Month 2+: Dependence — you can't fall asleep without it

⚠ IMPORTANT:

This is NOT our theory. It's a scientific consensus. That's why our alternatives (pink, brown, natural) are better.

🧠 WHY THE ALTERNATIVES ARE BETTER:

• Pink noise — fewer high frequencies, doesn't activate the alarm
• Brown noise — calming, deep
• Natural sounds — associated with positive emotions

📚 STUDY:

A 2023 comparative study (4-month follow-up): patients on white noise have 36% drop-out from treatment, while with personalized nature sounds the drop-out is only 16%. Patients literally can't tolerate white noise for a long time.
why_no_white_noise.source: Comparison of Enriched Acoustic Environment and White Noise study, 2024. See docs/research/11.

why_habituation.title: Why "habituation", not a cure?
why_habituation.micro: Tinnitus cannot be cured — it's a signal in the brain. But the brain can learn to ignore it. This is called habituation.
why_habituation.full:
📊 WHAT TINNITUS REALLY IS:

Tinnitus is NOT a sound in the ears. It's an electrical signal in the brain that the brain processes as sound. That's why ear surgery doesn't help.

🎯 WHY WE CAN'T CURE IT:

In 95% of cases tinnitus is caused by:
• Hearing loss (with age or after noise exposure)
• Stress and anxiety
• Changes in the nervous system

These changes are not reversible — the brain can't forget the wrong signal.

✅ WHAT ACTUALLY WORKS:

The brain can learn to IGNORE the tinnitus. In the same way that after a while you don't notice:
• A clock on the wall (ticking)
• The fridge in the kitchen
• The hum of the car

This is called HABITUATION.

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT FROM HABITUATION:

The tinnitus will STAY, but:

Month 1-2:
Less stress from it. Fewer "why me?" thoughts.

Month 3-6:
Minutes a day when you don't notice it.

Month 6-12:
Hours a day when you don't notice it. The tinnitus becomes like background noise.

⚠ IMPORTANT:

• The tinnitus won't disappear
• But the brain will learn not to react
• This frees you from stress and suffering

🧠 WHY AN APP LIKE AURALIS HELPS:

We help the brain to "re-tune". Sound enrichment + the right thoughts (CBT) teach the brain that the tinnitus IS NOT DANGEROUS.

📚 STUDY:

TRT (Tinnitus Retraining Therapy) — a methodology used since 1990 by Pawel Jastreboff. Over 80,000 patients worldwide. Results: 75-85% achieve habituation in 12-18 months with the right protocol.

what_is_di_score.title: What is DI (the Distress Index)?
what_is_di_score.micro: DI measures how much stress the tinnitus is causing you now. A scale from 0 to 20. The lower, the better.
what_is_di_score.full:
📊 WHAT DI IS:

DI = Distress Index. A scale from 0 to 20.

DI is calculated from 3 questions in our test:
1. How often do you think about the tinnitus?
2. How much does it interfere with your daily life?
3. What is the subjective level (0-10)?

🎯 WHY IT MATTERS:

DI tells us two things:
1. How much YOUR tinnitus interferes with you
2. Which sound mix is suitable for you

Not all tinnitus is the same. High DI needs a different sound approach than low DI.

📈 WHAT THE LEVELS MEAN:

DI 0-5: Mild
• The tinnitus is there, but rarely interferes
• Recommendation: faint sounds, only when needed

DI 6-12: Moderate
• The tinnitus interferes in quiet moments
• Recommendation: medium-loud sounds, daily

DI 13-20: High
• The tinnitus interferes every day
• Recommendation: strong masking sounds, long sessions

📊 HOW TO TRACK IT:

DI is measured again every month. Expect:

Month 1: DI drops by 1-2 points
Month 3: DI drops by 3-5 points
Month 6: DI drops by 5-10 points

This is a realistic progression according to clinical data.

⚠ IMPORTANT:

• DI is not the "severity" of the illness — it measures the IMPACT on your life
• Lowering DI = less suffering (even if the sound stays the same)

🧠 WHY WE USE DI:

DI is based on internationally validated scales (THI and TFI), but simplified for quick daily tracking. The minimal clinically significant improvement on THI is 7 points — our DI scale reflects this.

📚 SOURCE:

The DI scale was developed based on the Tinnitus Handicap Inventory (THI, Newman et al., 1996) — the gold standard in clinical practice, used since 1996 in over 50 countries.
what_is_di_score.source: Newman, C.W. et al. (1996). THI. See docs/research/18.

how_long_to_listen.title: How long should I listen each day?
how_long_to_listen.micro: At least 30 minutes a day. Ideally 60-90 minutes. Less = weak improvement. More = good, but not required.
how_long_to_listen.full:
📊 RECOMMENDED DURATION:

For significant results:
• MINIMUM: 30 minutes a day
• IDEAL: 60-90 minutes a day
• MAXIMUM: all night (for falling asleep) is safe

🎯 WHY THERE'S A MINIMUM:

The brain needs time to "learn" the new sound environment. Under 30 minutes a day — the effect is minimal.

It's like physical training: 5 minutes at the gym doesn't build muscle.

📈 EFFECT OF DIFFERENT DURATIONS:

15 minutes a day:
Small effect. DI improvement: 1-2 points per month.

30 minutes a day:
Moderate effect. DI improvement: 3-5 points per month.

60 minutes a day:
Strong effect. DI improvement: 5-8 points per month.

90+ minutes a day:
Maximum effect. DI improvement: 7-10 points per month.

⚠ WHEN IT'S DRIFT:

• Less than 15 minutes → you're wasting time
• More than 4 hours in a row → take a break (10-15 minutes)
• All night for sleep → safe

💡 PRACTICAL TIPS:

• Morning (15 min) + Before sleep (30 min) = a good combination
• During work (60 min background) — very effective
• At night as a background — safe for long sessions

🧠 WHY DAILY USE IS KEY:

Habituation of the brain is like learning a new word — it requires repetition. One intensive day is less effective than daily short sessions.

📚 STUDY:

A multicenter study (2024, 793 patients): patients with 60+ minutes of daily use achieve -24.6 points improvement on the THI scale over 6 months. That's 3.5 times more than the minimal clinically significant improvement (7 points).

why_no_volume_max.title: Why does the volume matter?
why_no_volume_max.micro: Don't play it at maximum! The recommended volume is one that BARELY masks the tinnitus. Loud = harmful to hearing.
why_no_volume_max.full:
📊 THE RIGHT SOUND LEVEL:

The sound should be:
✅ Loud enough that you hear it comfortably
✅ Just enough to MASK but not DROWN OUT the tinnitus
❌ NOT so loud that it drowns out everything around you

🎯 WHY SUCH A PRECISE BALANCE:

Too loud a sound:
1. May damage your hearing (especially above 60 dB)
2. Doesn't help more than a medium-loud sound
3. Tires the brain after hours
4. May make the tinnitus LOUDER in the long term

Too quiet a sound:
1. Doesn't mask the tinnitus
2. You hear the tinnitus louder
3. Stress

📈 "MIXING POINT" — THE IDEAL LEVEL:

In clinical practice the concept of the "mixing point" is used — the point where you hear BOTH the sound AND the tinnitus, but the sound predominates.

How to find it:
1. Start quietly
2. Slowly increase the volume
3. Stop when the tinnitus becomes "pleasantly covered" but NOT fully drowned out

⚠ IMPORTANT:

• Do NOT increase the volume to turn off the tinnitus completely
• This activates a defensive reaction of the brain
• The tinnitus will come back louder when you stop

💡 PRACTICAL TIP:

Recommended phone volume: 30-40% (no more than 50%).

With headphones: ALWAYS quieter than without headphones — the sound goes directly into the ear.

🧠 WHY THIS TECHNIQUE:

The goal of sound enrichment is NOT to drown out the tinnitus. The goal is to give the brain an ALTERNATIVE sound to focus on. This allows habituation.

📚 STUDY:

The Phase II LINTS study (2023): the best results were achieved with BARELY perceptible sounds (at the threshold of hearing) — patients often don't even realize it's playing, but the brain re-tunes.

CAUTION: Long-term exposure above 70 dB may damage hearing. Recommended maximum headphone volume: 60% of the device maximum.
why_no_volume_max.source: Phase II LINTS, 2023 + WHO Safe Listening Guidelines. See docs/research/11 + docs/research/13.

cat_underwater.title: Underwater sounds
cat_underwater.micro: Deep, enveloping sounds like being underwater. Between 60 and 300 hertz. Safest for people with high-pitched tinnitus.
cat_underwater.full:
📊 WHAT THEY ARE:

Sounds that remind you of how you hear when you are underwater in a pool or lake. Very deep frequencies (60-300 hertz) with no high "whistling" tones.

🎯 WHO THEY ARE FOR:
✅ High-pitched whistling tinnitus (TH_C) — best coverage
✅ Stress-sensitive people
✅ For falling asleep (especially)
✅ For people who love solitude
⚠ Not for people whose tinnitus is precisely in the bass range

💡 WHEN:
• Before sleep (highly recommended)
• During the night as background
• During anxiety or a panic attack
• During quiet work or reading

📈 WHY THESE SOUNDS WERE CHOSEN:

Each of our underwater sounds is tested with scientific analysis:
• Spectral centroid below 350 hertz
• No high peaks above 4000 hertz
• Loop-friendly (quiet at the edges for a seamless transition)
• Score 100/100 on our tinnitus-safety algorithm

🧠 WHY THEY WORK:

Deep frequencies below 300Hz activate the calming nervous system. They "overlap" the high tones of tinnitus (which are usually 4000-8000Hz). This breaks the "tinnitus → stress" link.

📚 STUDY:

A study of acoustic environments (EAE vs WN, 2024): enriched deep sounds show 50% less drop-out from the program compared to traditional white noise (16% vs 36% drop-out over 4 months).
cat_underwater.source: EAE vs White Noise 4-Month Feasibility Study, 2024. See docs/research/11.

cat_ocean.title: Ocean and sea sounds
cat_ocean.micro: Wave sounds and distant sea scenes. Natural sounds associated with rest and calm.
cat_ocean.full:
📊 WHAT THEY ARE:

Real recordings of seashores — distant waves, quiet surf, a seaside-holiday atmosphere. Not a "storm at sea", but a calm sea.

🎯 WHO THEY ARE FOR:
✅ Stress-sensitive profile (SS_R)
✅ Somatic/fluctuating profile (SM_F)
✅ People with positive associations to the sea
⚠ Not for people with a fear of water or sea-related trauma

💡 WHEN:
• In the morning on waking (15-30 min)
• After work to relax
• During the day as an ambient background
• When you need a "mental holiday"

📈 WHY THESE SOUNDS:

Not all ocean sounds are the same. Ours are specially chosen:
• No strong waves or surf (sharp transients)
• No gulls or birds (high peaks)
• No people or machines
• A purely natural scene

🧠 WHY THEY WORK:

Natural water sounds activate brain areas linked to positive emotions and memories. This is a form of emotional habituation — the brain associates tinnitus with a pleasant memory, not with anxiety.

📚 STUDY:

A comparative study (Tong et al., 2023, 120 patients): personalized natural sounds show half the drop-out of traditional sound therapy. 73% of patients reach a clinically meaningful improvement after 12 weeks.
cat_ocean.source: Tong et al. (2023) + TENT-A1/A2 trials, 2024. See docs/research/11.

cat_river.title: Rivers and streams
cat_river.micro: Underwater and above-water sounds from rivers. Mid-frequency (1000-2000Hz). Ideal for concentration and work.
cat_river.full:
📊 WHAT THEY ARE:

Recordings of mountain rivers, brooks and streams — from an underwater perspective (softer) and above water (with more detail). Between medium-soft and medium-bright sounds.

🎯 WHO THEY ARE FOR:
✅ Low-frequency noise-type tinnitus (DN_S)
✅ Adapted/mild profile (HB_M)
✅ People who have used sound therapy for a long time
✅ For concentration and work
⚠ Not for people with tinnitus in the mid frequencies (1000-3000Hz)

💡 WHEN:
• During work (60-120 min)
• While studying or reading
• After a stressful day
• Less suitable for night-time listening

📈 WHY THESE SOUNDS:

Rivers are in the mid-frequency range, which perfectly "overlaps" the zone where many tinnitus tones live. They also have natural variations that do not tire the brain the way constant white noise does.

🧠 WHY THEY WORK:

Mid-frequency sounds (1000-2000Hz) directly mask the most common tinnitus frequencies. The natural fluctuations in the current create a "sound texture" that the brain finds pleasant.

📚 STUDY:

A multicentre study (2024, n=793): enriched natural sounds (including rivers) show 84% better treatment adherence from patients compared to traditional white noise.

cat_meditation.title: Meditative compositions
cat_meditation.micro: Long, designed ambient pieces. Between 5 and 15 minutes each. For deep relaxation and meditation.
cat_meditation.full:
📊 WHAT THEY ARE:

Carefully chosen compositions by professional artists. No words, no rhythm, no melody in the traditional sense — just a long, slowly changing soundscape.

🎯 WHO THEY ARE FOR:
✅ For long meditative sessions (5-15 min)
✅ For people who already practise meditation
✅ For deep relaxation under stress
✅ Universal — most profiles
⚠ May be "too deep" for active moments of the day

💡 WHEN:
• Before sleep (especially the 15-minute Aquatic Meditation)
• For structured meditative sessions
• During strong stress or panic episodes
• We do NOT recommend them as a background for work (too immersive)

📈 WHY THESE COMPOSITIONS:

Each composition is tested:
• No abrupt transitions
• No high peaks (>4kHz)
• Long enough for full relaxation
• Score 80-100 on our algorithm

🧠 WHY THEY WORK:

Long compositions allow the brain to enter a deeper state. After 7-10 minutes, changes in brain waves begin (alpha and theta), which is the state of deep relaxation.

📚 STUDY:

Research on music therapy and tinnitus (2020-2024): long ambient compositions show better results for sleep quality than short sounds. Especially when used as part of an evening ritual.
cat_meditation.source: Music therapy for tinnitus review, 2020-2024. See docs/research/11 + docs/research/09.

cat_forest.title: Forest sounds
cat_forest.micro: Winter forest, wet leaves, distant rain in the forest. Carefully chosen WITHOUT birds (which can trigger tinnitus).
cat_forest.full:
📊 WHAT THEY ARE:

Natural forest scenes — winter conditions with soft snow, wet leaves, distant murmuring sounds. Calm, without dramatic elements.

🎯 WHO THEY ARE FOR:
✅ Adapted/mild profile (HB_M)
✅ Stress-sensitive (SS_R)
✅ People who love nature
✅ For psychological respite
⚠ Not for people with a phobia of forests or nature

💡 WHEN:
• During the day as an ambient background
• While working at the computer
• After urban tension
• Less suitable for night-time listening

📈 WHY THESE SPECIFIC SOUNDS:

ATTENTION: Most "forest" recordings have birds. Bird trills are in the HIGH frequencies (3000-8000Hz) — exactly where many people's tinnitus is. This can aggravate the problem.

Our forest sounds are:
• Winter conditions (fewer birds)
• Wetlands (wet forests, more muffled)
• No birdsong as a main element
• Tested for safety

🧠 WHY THEY WORK:

Natural sounds activate the prefrontal cortex — the part of the brain linked to positive emotions and calm. This is the direct opposite of the anxiety that sustains tinnitus.

📚 STUDY:

Sound therapy research review (2024): natural sounds show better compensation and less drop-out than artificial ones (white noise). 84% better treatment adherence after 3 months.

cat_base_noise.title: Base noises (Pink, Brown, Green)
cat_base_noise.micro: Mathematically generated noises — the classic in sound enrichment for tinnitus. Pink, brown, green — each with a different spectral profile.
cat_base_noise.full:
📊 WHAT THEY ARE:

Pure, "technical" noises, computed with mathematical formulas. They do not come from the real world — they are generated in real time by your phone.

🎯 THE THREE TYPES:

PINK NOISE:
• Balanced — more lows than highs
• Like steady rain
• A universal choice
• A classic of 30+ years in TRT therapy

BROWN NOISE:
• Bassier than pink
• Like heavy rain or a distant waterfall
• Ideal for falling asleep
• Recommended for high-pitched whistling tinnitus

GREEN NOISE:
• Mid-frequency filtered
• Focused around 500Hz
• More focused than pink
• Good for concentration

💡 WHEN TO USE WHICH:

• Pink — to start, universal
• Brown — for falling asleep, night
• Green — for work, focus

📈 WHY THESE SOUNDS ARE STILL RELEVANT:

Even though we have natural sounds, the base noises remain useful:
• Constant predictability (no variations)
• A perfectly even spectrum
• They do not tire attention
• They contain no semantic information (like birds or people)

🧠 WHY THEY WORK:

Base noises have been studied since the 1970s. Pink and Brown are the foundation of Tinnitus Retraining Therapy (TRT) — a methodology used since 1990 by Pawel Jastreboff.

📚 STUDY:

TRT clinical practice over 30 years, 80,000+ patients worldwide. 75-85% reach habituation with correct application. Pink and Brown noise are recommended as the base for any sound protocol.
cat_base_noise.source: Jastreboff TRT model, 1990+ + Web Audio API research. See docs/research/02 + docs/research/11.

nav_mixer_intro.title: What is the "Mixer"?
nav_mixer_intro.micro: The heart of AURALIS. Here you play sounds that help with tinnitus — individually or combined into recommended mixes.
nav_mixer_intro.full:
📊 WHAT THE MIXER IS:

The Mixer (mix) is the main tool in AURALIS. Here you:
• Listen to recommended mixes (ready-made sound combinations)
• Find individual sounds by category
• Control the volume
• Set a sleep timer

🎯 HOW TO START:

First steps:
1. Open "Recommended" — there are mixes chosen especially for your profile
2. Tap the star ⭐ — this is the most recommended one for you
3. Listen for 30-60 minutes
4. Save your favourites

📈 LAYOUT:

Tab 1 "Recommended" (3-5 mixes):
• Based on your Quiz answers
• ⭐ means "most suitable for your profile"
• Start with these

Tab 2 "All sounds":
• 30+ individual sounds by category
• Underwater, ocean, rivers, meditations, forest, base
• For exploring and experimenting

💡 PRACTICAL TIPS:

• Do not change the mix too often (minimum 10 min)
• Give the brain time to accept the sound
• Experiment with different ones at different times of day

⚠ IMPORTANT:

The Mixer works best with regular use. 30 minutes a day is the minimum for meaningful results.

📚 STUDY:

Clinical data show: daily use of sound enrichment (minimum 30 min) for 3 months achieves an average improvement of 8.6 points on the THI scale.
nav_mixer_intro.source: Sound therapy duration studies, 2020-2024. See docs/research/11.

nav_calm_intro.title: What is "Calm"?
nav_calm_intro.micro: Audio-guided relaxation exercises. Breathing, muscle relaxation and accepting the tinnitus.
nav_calm_intro.full:
📊 WHAT CALM IS:

The Calm section is the "psychological" side of AURALIS. While the Mixer works with sound enrichment, Calm works with psychological techniques.

🎯 WHAT IS INCLUDED (beta 1.0):

Three short exercises:

1. 4-7-8 breathing (3 minutes)
• Inhale for 4 seconds
• Hold for 7 seconds
• Exhale for 8 seconds
• Reduces stress instantly

2. Progressive muscle relaxation (5 minutes)
• Systematic release of muscles
• From feet to head
• Especially good before sleep

3. Accepting the noise (4 minutes)
• Based on ACT therapy (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy)
• Teaches the brain that tinnitus is NOT a threat
• Reduces stress in the long term

💡 HOW TO USE THEM:

• One exercise a day is enough
• A different exercise at different moments:
  - Breathing → during stress in the day
  - PMR → before sleep
  - Acceptance → when the tinnitus worries you

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT:

Week 1-2: Less stress instantly
Week 3-4: Fewer negative thoughts about the tinnitus
Month 2-3: Greater psychological acceptance

⚠ IMPORTANT:

These techniques will NOT remove the tinnitus. They will change your attitude towards it.

📚 STUDY:

A systematic review of CBT (cognitive behavioural therapy) for tinnitus: 65-75% of patients report a meaningful improvement in quality of life, even when the loudness of the tinnitus stays the same.
nav_calm_intro.source: CBT for tinnitus systematic review, 2024. See docs/research/07 + docs/research/08 + docs/research/21.

nav_diary_intro.title: What is the "Diary"?
nav_diary_intro.micro: A daily record of sleep and tinnitus level. Helps you see your progress in numbers.
nav_diary_intro.full:
📊 WHAT THE DIARY IS:

A short daily record (1 minute a day). You log:
• How many hours you slept
• How well you slept (1-5)
• Tinnitus level (0-10)
• A short note if you wish

🎯 WHY IT MATTERS:

Without numbers you cannot see progress. People with tinnitus often do not notice the small improvements — they happen slowly (over months).

The diary helps you to:
• See the trend (with a chart)
• Find patterns (when it is worse, when better)
• Correlate with medical visits

💡 HOW TO USE IT:

• In the morning (after waking) — for the night
• A record in under a minute
• No writing — just buttons

📈 WHAT TO EXPECT:

After 14 days you will have enough data for the first charts.
After 30 days you will see clear patterns.
After 60 days you will see the long-term trend.

⚠ IMPORTANT:

Tinnitus varies naturally. One bad day does not mean nothing is working. Look at the 7-day average, not single days.

📚 STUDY:

The clinical assessment of sleep quality (Consensus Sleep Diary) is the gold standard in tinnitus research. Daily tracking makes it possible to catch an improvement of 7+ points on the THI, which is the minimal clinically meaningful change.
nav_diary_intro.source: Consensus Sleep Diary methodology + THI MCID studies. See docs/research/10 + docs/research/18.

sleep_timer_explained.title: What is the "Sleep timer"?
sleep_timer_explained.micro: Automatically stops the sound after a chosen time. Safe for the whole night — the sound fades out smoothly in the last 5 seconds.
sleep_timer_explained.full:
📊 WHAT IT DOES:

You set a time (15, 30, 60, 90, 120 minutes) → the sound stops automatically → the phone saves battery.

🎯 WHY IT MATTERS:

• Safety — it does not play all night if you did not want it to
• Battery — saves power
• Hearing — reduces total exposure

💡 PRACTICAL TIPS:

For falling asleep:
• Choose 30-60 minutes
• That is enough to fall asleep
• If you wake up — start it again

For the whole night:
• Choose 120 minutes OR
• Turn the timer off (risky for the battery)

📈 TECHNICAL DETAILS:

The sound does not stop abruptly. The last 5 seconds:
• Gradually decreasing volume
• No abrupt cut-off
• The brain does not receive a "shock" signal

⚠ IMPORTANT:

Abruptly stopping sound enrichment can cause a "rebound effect" — the tinnitus sounding louder than it was before listening. That is why the fade-out is critical.

📚 STUDY:

Sleep research shows: tinnitus patients who use a sleep timer (vs all night) report better sleep quality. Excessively long night-time exposure can lead to dependence.
sleep_timer_explained.source: Sleep + sound therapy research. See docs/research/09.

theme_toggle_explained.title: Why two themes (dark and light)?
theme_toggle_explained.micro: Dark is for evening and night (less strain on the eyes). Light is for the day. You can switch at any time.
theme_toggle_explained.full:
📊 WHAT IT DOES:

The button at the top right switches between:
• Dark theme — for evening, night, quiet work
• Light theme — for the day, bright light

🎯 WHY YOU HAVE A CHOICE:

Dark theme (evening/night):
• Less blue light (which disrupts sleep)
• Less strain on the eyes
• Less "waking up" before sleep

Light theme (day):
• Better readability in bright light
• More natural for daytime hours
• A less "sleepy" atmosphere

💡 RECOMMENDATION:

• Morning and during the day → Light
• After sunset → Dark
• Before sleep → Dark (mandatory)

📈 WHY IT MATTERS FOR TINNITUS:

Poor sleep worsens tinnitus. Blue light from screens (especially in the evening) disrupts melatonin and sleep.

The dark theme reduces blue light and helps the body prepare for sleep. This improves sleep quality → reduces tinnitus.

📚 STUDY:

Research on blue light and sleep: exposure to blue light 2 hours before sleep reduces melatonin production by 23%. In tinnitus patients, poor sleep is directly linked to a higher THI score.
theme_toggle_explained.source: Blue light + sleep research + tinnitus sleep studies. See docs/research/09.

profile_recommendation_explained.title: Why are these mixes recommended?
profile_recommendation_explained.micro: They are based on your Quiz answers. Different tinnitus needs different sounds. Our recommendations are personalized.
profile_recommendation_explained.full:
📊 HOW THE RECOMMENDATION WORKS:

In the Quiz you answered 15 questions. The system calculates:
1. The type of your tinnitus (5 possible profiles)
2. Stress index (0-20)
3. Recommended sounds

🎯 THE FIVE PROFILES:

TH_C - Tonal high-frequency:
• A whistling or chirping sound
• High frequency (8000Hz+)
• Recommendation: deep masking sounds

DN_S - Noise-type low-frequency:
• Humming or rumbling
• Low frequency
• Recommendation: mid-frequency sounds

SS_R - Stress-sensitive:
• Reacts strongly to stress
• Wavers over time
• Recommendation: natural relaxing sounds

SM_F - Somatic/Fluctuating:
• Linked to the body (neck, jaw)
• Changes with position
• Recommendation: varied sounds

HB_M - Adapted/Mild:
• Already less anxious
• Greater control
• Recommendation: experimenting with different ones

💡 WHY IT MATTERS:

The wrong mix = you waste time. The right mix = faster improvement.

For example:
• High-pitched tinnitus + deep sound = EXCELLENT masking
• High-pitched tinnitus + high sound = WRONG (intensifies the tinnitus)

📈 WHAT ⭐ MEANS:

The star marks a VERY suitable mix for your profile. We recommend you start with it.

⚠ IMPORTANT:

The recommendation is based on average data from studies. Every person is individual. Experiment — you may find that another mix works better for you.

📚 STUDY:

A study (Tong et al., 2023): personalized recommendations based on tinnitus characteristics lead to twice as good treatment adherence as standard recommendations.
profile_recommendation_explained.source: Tong et al. (2023) + Quiz validation studies. See docs/research/01 + docs/research/11.

star_icon_explained.title: What does the star mean?
star_icon_explained.micro: The star marks the most suitable mix for your profile. It is based on your Quiz answers.
star_icon_explained.full:
📊 WHAT IT MEANS:

The star = "Most recommended for you"

Not all 5 recommended mixes are equal. The star marks the TOP-1 according to your profile.

🎯 HOW IT IS CHOSEN:

The algorithm takes into account:
• Type of tinnitus (from the Quiz)
• Loudness of tinnitus (from the DI)
• Sensitivity to stress
• Duration preferences

It then prioritizes the mix with the strongest overlap with your needs.

💡 PRACTICAL MEANING:

I recommend you:
1. Start with the star mix (the first 7 days)
2. Then try the other recommended ones
3. See what works best for YOU

⚠ IMPORTANT:

The star is a recommendation, not a law. If the sound does not appeal to you or does not help after 7 days — try another.

📚 STUDY:

Personalized recommendations in sound therapy show 2x better effectiveness than standard ones (Tong et al., 2023, 120 patients).
star_icon_explained.source: Tong et al. (2023) + personalization studies. See docs/research/01 + docs/research/05.

why_no_medical_advice.title: Why don't we recommend medicines or supplements?
why_no_medical_advice.micro: AURALIS is a wellness tool, not a medical product. Medicines and supplements are recommended by a doctor, not by an app.
why_no_medical_advice.full:
📊 WHAT AURALIS IS:

AURALIS is a WELLNESS app. We help with:
✅ Sound enrichment
✅ Psychological techniques (CBT, mindfulness)
✅ Education about tinnitus
✅ Progress tracking

🚨 WHAT AURALIS IS NOT:

AURALIS is NOT:
❌ A medical product
❌ A doctor
❌ A replacement for a medical consultation
❌ A source of medicine recommendations

🎯 WHY WE DO NOT GIVE MEDICAL ADVICE:

1. Safety - every person is different. What works for one may harm another.
2. Regulation - regulators require medical products to pass strict certification. We are wellness, not medical.
3. Trust - a real doctor knows you personally. We do not.

💡 WHEN TO SEE A DOCTOR:

✅ First time with tinnitus (to rule out illness)
✅ Tinnitus in only one ear
✅ Tinnitus with dizziness
✅ Tinnitus with hearing loss
✅ Tinnitus after trauma
✅ Tinnitus with depression or thoughts of self-harm

📈 WHAT WE RECOMMEND:

Find a specialist:
• AN ENT DOCTOR (otorhinolaryngologist)
• AN AUDIOLOGIST
• A PSYCHOLOGIST (for severe stress)

⚠ IMPORTANT CONCLUSION:

AURALIS works BEST when combined with medical care. The doctor takes care of the body. We help with the daily coping.

📚 STUDY:

Regulatory analysis (2024): wellness products WITHOUT medical claims have better long-term acceptance and trust from users.
why_no_medical_advice.source: Regulatory analysis for digital tinnitus tools, 2024. See docs/research/16.
